plan.reasoning: Round 1 should split effort between the two indispensable halves. The lower bound seems within reach if we prove a PL bend-circle lemma and a weak T-pattern metric lemma that tolerates fan degeneracies. The upper bound is category-sensitive, so a separate solver should either make the anisotropic-scaling construction rigorous or isolate the exact PL approximation lemma needed.
plan.strategic_note: We are trying first to prove beta realized implies beta > sqrt(3) via PL apex-to-ridge leaves, oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam, and a weak version of Schwartz's trapezoid metric argument. In parallel, seek a rigorous beta > sqrt(3) construction using anisotropic scaling of a paper Mobius band plus a clean affine triangulation; this is likely the harder exact-category issue.

new_bottlenecks: Need a rigorous proof that the quotient leaf space of apex-to-ridge segments is a single circle and that the induced oriented image-line path reverses orientation after one circuit.
Need to handle T-pattern degeneracies caused by multiple leaves in one clean triangle sharing an apex.
Need an upper construction in the exact class: affine on clean triangles, strict ridge contraction, strict side expansion, and quotient injectivity.
kb_updates.frontier: Establish the lower bound in the PL setting, preferably by proving that any perpendicular intersecting pair of image support lines from the bend circle, even if sharing a boundary endpoint, yields the same beta > sqrt(3) inequalities.

task_assignments.description: Prove lower bound via PL bend circle and weak T-pattern
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are Solver L. Goal: prove the lower bound for the following problem: if β is realized, then β > sqrt(3). Work self-contained and be very precise about PL/topological details.

Problem data: Sigma = R x [0,1], boundary dSigma = R x {0,1}, Gβ(x,y)=(x+β,1-y). A clean triangle is the convex hull of 3 non-collinear boundary points; it has one boundary edge, called its ridge, and one opposite boundary vertex, called its apex. A clean triangulation is countable locally finite with disjoint interiors covering Sigma and is Gβ-invariant. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is affine into R^3, strictly shortening the ridge and strictly lengthening the two other edges. Realized means there is continuous f:Sigma to R^3 affine squeeze on each triangle of a Gβ-invariant clean triangulation, and f(p)=f(q) iff p=Gβ^k(q) for some integer k.

Suggested route:
1. Descend to the quotient Mβ=Sigma/<Gβ>. Show f descends to a PL embedding F:Mβ to R^3 and the quotient triangulation is finite.
2. Prove the squeeze-cevian lemma: in one clean triangle with apex a and ridge endpoints r0,r1, if the affine image shortens r0r1 and lengthens ar0, ar1, then for every r=(1-s)r0+s r1 on the ridge, the segment ar is strictly lengthened. Hint: q(s)=|F(a)-F(r)|^2-|a-r|^2 has positive endpoint values and q''=2(|F(r1)-F(r0)|^2-|r1-r0|^2)<0.
3. Build the PL leaf space: each clean triangle contributes an interval of apex-to-ridge leaves; endpoints are its two non-boundary edges; glue interval endpoints across shared non-boundary edges. Prove this quotient is a single circle, not just a union of circles. Be careful at boundary vertices and use that the quotient is a connected triangulated Mobius band.
4. Lift this leaf circle to the infinite strip. Orient each lifted leaf from y=0 to y=1. Prove that as one goes once around the quotient leaf circle the corresponding oriented image support line L_t satisfies L_1=L_0^opp, because Gβ swaps y=0 and y=1 while f is Gβ-invariant. Establish continuity of t -> L_t through side-edge identifications.
5. Prove or quote with proof the oriented-line lemma: if L_t, t in [0,1], is a continuous family of oriented lines in R^3 with L_1=L_0^opp, then some L_r and L_s are perpendicular intersecting lines. A Borsuk-Ulam proof using dot product and moment/mixed product is fine.
6. Develop the metric endgame in a weak form allowing the two domain leaves T and B to share one boundary endpoint. Let T and B be the leaves whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Because F is an embedding, the image segments cannot cross in their relative interiors; label the two leaves so the image segment B lies in a closed ray from the line-intersection point P on its support line. Let q be the endpoint of B farther from P, so the distance from F(q) to the support line of F(T) is at least |F(B)|.
7. For T choose a lift from bottom to top with signed horizontal displacement t chosen so that the boundary arc D from the endpoints of T containing q has flat length β+t and the other boundary arc H has flat length β-t. Prove carefully that this is valid; in particular show any leaf has |t|<β, e.g. if |t|>β then the leaf intersects a Gβ-translate in the interior, contradicting the clean triangulation or injectivity.
8. Use boundary contraction and the cevian lemma to prove β-t = length_domain(H) > length_image(H) > |F(T)| > sqrt(1+t^2), hence β > sqrt(1+t^2)+t. Justify the strict inequality length_image(H) > |F(T)| from embeddedness: equality would force the boundary arc image to be exactly the straight segment F(T), impossible except at endpoints.
9. Use D: its image path connects the endpoints of F(T) and passes through F(q). Let base length L=|F(T)|>sqrt(1+t^2), and let y=dist(F(q), line F(T)) >= |F(B)| > length_flat(B) >= 1. By the reflected-triangle estimate, the portion of image(D) through F(q) has length at least sqrt(L^2+4y^2), hence length_domain(D)=β+t > sqrt(5+t^2). Conclude β > sqrt(5+t^2)-t.
10. Finish by proving max(sqrt(1+t^2)+t, sqrt(5+t^2)-t) >= sqrt(3), with equality at t=1/sqrt(3). Therefore β>sqrt(3).

If any step fails, identify the exact gap and propose a repair. In particular, do not ignore possible fan degeneracies; either prove the weak metric lemma covers them or state precisely what remains.

Justify the strict inequality length_image(H) > |F(T)| from embeddedness: equality would force the boundary arc image to be exactly the straight segment F(T), impossible except at endpoints.
9. Use D: its image path connects the endpoints of F(T) and passes through F(q). Let base length L=|F(T)|>sqrt(1+t^2), and let y=dist(F(q), line F(T)) >= |F(B)| > length_flat(B) >= 1. By the reflected-triangle estimate, the portion of image(D) through F(q) has length at least sqrt(L^2+4y^2), hence length_domain(D)=β+t > sqrt(5+t^2). Conclude β > sqrt(5+t^2)-t.
10. Finish by proving max(sqrt(1+t^2)+t, sqrt(5+t^2)-t) >= sqrt(3), with equality at t=1/sqrt(3). Therefore β>sqrt(3).

If any step fails, identify the exact gap and propose a repair. In particular, do not ignore possible fan degeneracies; either prove the weak metric lemma covers them or state precisely what remains.

task_assignments.description: Construct realizations for every beta > sqrt(3) in the exact clean PL category
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are Solver U. Goal: prove the upper bound for the problem: for every β>sqrt(3), β is realized. The exact category matters: we need a Gβ-invariant clean triangulation of Sigma=R x [0,1] and a continuous f:Sigma to R^3, affine on each clean triangle, strictly shortening every boundary ridge edge and strictly lengthening the two transverse sides, with f(p)=f(q) iff p=Gβ^k(q).

Problem definitions: Gβ(x,y)=(x+β,1-y). A clean triangle is the convex hull of 3 non-collinear points in R x {0,1}. A clean triangulation is countable locally finite, Gβ-invariant, pairwise disjoint interiors, union Sigma. A squeeze map on a clean triangle strictly shortens the boundary edge and strictly lengthens the other two edges.

Preferred strategy to make rigorous:
1. Choose constants a<1<c with μ=aβ/c > sqrt(3). This is possible because β>sqrt(3).
2. Work in a target flat Mobius band of width c and length L=aβ, aspect ratio μ=L/c>sqrt(3). Seek an embedded PL paper Mobius band F0 from this target band to R^3, with a clean triangulation, affine on triangles, quotient-injective, boundary edges not longer than their target flat lengths, and transverse side edges at least their target flat lengths. Ideally make F0 isometric on the relevant edges.
3. Pull back by A(x,y)=(a x, c y), which conjugates Gβ to the glide on the target dimensions: A(x+β,1-y)=(A_x+L,c-A_y). Boundary edge lengths in the domain are multiplied by a in the target, so after F0 they are strictly shorter than the original if a<1.
4. A transverse side with target horizontal span ΔX has target flat length sqrt(ΔX^2+c^2), while its preimage in Sigma has length sqrt((ΔX/a)^2+1). If the clean triangulation is refined so |ΔX|<δ and δ^2(a^{-2}-1)<c^2-1, then sqrt(ΔX^2+c^2)>sqrt((ΔX/a)^2+1), giving strict side expansion.

Your job is to fill the missing existence and approximation details. You may use known existence of smooth embedded paper Mobius bands for every aspect ratio >sqrt(3), but if you do, you must explain how to pass to a clean affine triangulation preserving embedding and the strict edge inequalities above. A plausible route is: take a smooth embedded developable paper band with a ruling foliation, choose many ruling endpoints on the boundary, build a fine boundary-vertex triangulation whose transverse edges are close to rulings or are controlled by the c>1 slack, and use a PL approximation theorem preserving embeddings. However, clean triangles have all vertices on the boundary and transverse sides span the full width, so do not handwave this point.

Alternative route: give an explicit finite PL construction, for example an opened version of the triangular Mobius band, then pull back as above. If using a triangular/pleated model, provide coordinates for vertices, the quotient identifications, the clean triangulation, and verify all edge inequalities and injectivity.

Deliver either a complete construction or a precise conditional lemma of the form: if a PL paper Mobius band with properties X exists for all aspect μ>sqrt(3), then β is realized; then prove the conditional implication fully and state exactly how X can be obtained or what remains.

plan.reasoning: The lower-bound proof in t1 appears conceptually complete but contains several delicate PL/topological points: the leaf graph, orientation reversal, and T-pattern degeneracies should be independently audited. The upper proof in t2 is rigorous as a reduction, but the main remaining issue is removing or precisely justifying the smooth ruled paper-band input and checking there is no category mismatch when passing to invariant clean PL squeeze triangulations.
plan.strategic_note: Treat the lower bound as nearly done but send it to a red-team/proof-repair agent before commissioning a final write-up. For the upper bound, focus on either a self-contained Sadowsky/Halpern-Weaver construction plus the t2 discretization, or a direct resolved triangular PL model.

new_proven_results.statement: In the equispaced three-boundary-vertex triangular model for the quotient Möbius band, the three boundary arcs have flat length 2β/3 and the natural transverse chord edges have flat length sqrt(1+β^2/9), so there is a target edge length s strictly between transverse and boundary lengths if and only if β>sqrt(3).
new_proven_results.sketch: Take boundary vertices represented by (0,0), (2β/3,0), and (β/3,1). Boundary separation along the quotient boundary is 2β/3 for each adjacent pair. The natural transverse representatives have horizontal displacement β/3 and vertical displacement 1, hence length sqrt(1+β^2/9). The inequality sqrt(1+β^2/9)<2β/3 is equivalent to β^2>3.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Map the naive three-vertex triangular Möbius Δ-complex directly to one equilateral triangle.
new_failed_attempts.reason: Even though the edge-length slack exists for β>sqrt(3), each boundary edge and the corresponding interior edge with the same endpoint pair have the same affine image chord, so quotient injectivity fails unless the model is subdivided or resolved in 3D.

new_bottlenecks: The upper bound still depends on a smooth full-width ruled paper-band existence theorem or on constructing an explicit embedded clean PL resolution of the triangular model.
The lower bound should be independently checked for PL degeneracies, especially leaves in the same triangle sharing an apex and support-line intersections outside the actual image segments.
kb_updates.frontier: Obtain an unconditional upper construction for every β>sqrt(3), while red-teaming the PL lower-bound proof so it can be safely written up.

task_assignments.description: Red-team and repair the PL lower-bound proof
task_assignments.solver_prompt: You are a red-team/proof-repair agent for the lower bound in the clean-triangle PL Möbius-band problem. The attached output t1_lower_PL_T_pattern is a proposed proof that every realized β satisfies β>sqrt(3). Your job is to audit it rigorously and, if needed, repair it into a watertight proof. Check especially: (1) the quotient triangulation is finite and honest PL, and the descended map F is an embedding; (2) the squeeze-cevian lemma and the claim that every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened; (3) the construction and connectedness of the leaf graph, including boundary-vertex fans and possible Δ-complex edge identifications; (4) continuity of the oriented image support-line family through side-edge identifications and reversal after one deck circuit; (5) the oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma, including the moment-coordinate incidence condition; (6) the metric endgame in all degeneracies: leaves in the same triangle sharing an apex, leaves sharing one boundary endpoint, support-line intersection outside one or both image segments, and the claim that the chosen point q is not an endpoint of T; (7) the boundary arc formulas β±t and the proof that |t|<β. If a step fails, produce a corrected argument. If the proof works, produce a polished theorem proof with clearly stated lemmas and strict inequalities. End with a SOLVER_REPORT listing proven_results, failed_attempts, and progress_notes.
task_assignments._solver_prompt_full:
# Referenced Prior Solver Outputs
Below are the full outputs of the prior tasks the orchestrator asked you to read. Each section starts with a metadata line that tells you whether the artifact has been verified by an independent verifier+refiner: a `verified ✓` block is the canonical, refined text and its claim/relaxation flags are authoritative; an `unverified self-report` block is a raw draft whose claim flags are the agent's own self-declaration and have NOT been checked. Examine the actual arguments — do not rely solely on summaries elsewhere in this prompt.

## Task t1_lower_PL_T_pattern — Prove lower bound via PL bend circle and weak T-pattern
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I will assume \(\beta>0\), as is implicit in the claimed bound. If negative \(\beta\)’s are allowed, conjugating by \((x,y)\mapsto(-x,y)\) changes \(\beta\) to \(|\beta|\), so the natural statement is \(|\beta|>\sqrt3\).

## Theorem

If \(\beta\) is realized, then \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

---

## 1. Quotient, finiteness, and PL embedding

Let \(\mathcal T\) be the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\). The action is free and properly discontinuous, and

\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
\]

is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\). Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Since realization gives

\[
f(p)=f(q)\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]

for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), \(f\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant and descends to a continuous injective map

\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]

Because \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need that the quotient triangulation is finite and honest PL. For \(k\neq0\), every point is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) by distance at least \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were non-injective on a triangle \(\Delta\), then, since \(f|_\Delta\) is affine, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\), hence \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\), impossible. Thus \(f|_\Delta\) is injective on every triangle. Consequently no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself: if \(z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity on \(\Delta\).

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, so there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of triangles. Hence the quotient is a finite PL triangulation/cell-triangulation of the Möbius band, and \(F\) is PL affine on each quotient triangle.

---

## 2. Squeeze-cevian lemma

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle with apex \(a\) and ridge \([r_0,r_1]\). Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For

\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+s r_1,\qquad s\in[0,1],
\]

define

\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]

The squeeze assumptions give

\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0,
\]

because the two apex edges are strictly lengthened. Since \(A\) is affine,

\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]

because the ridge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, so for \(0\le s\le1\),

\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]

Therefore every cevian from the apex to the ridge is strictly lengthened:

\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]

In particular, every leaf segment has nonzero image length.

Also, every boundary edge is a ridge, so every boundary edge is strictly shortened. Since \(f\) is affine on each boundary edge, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in path length.

---

## 3. Leaf graph is one circle

For each clean triangle, take an abstract interval parametrizing its apex-to-ridge cevians. The two endpoints of this interval correspond to the two non-boundary edges. Glue endpoints whenever the corresponding non-boundary edges are the same edge of the triangulation. Call the resulting graph \(\Lambda_M\) in the quotient.

Every non-boundary edge of a triangulated surface is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles. We prove it is connected.

Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all quotient triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). If a triangle of \(U_C\) meets another triangle across a non-boundary edge, then those two triangle-intervals share a vertex in \(\Lambda_M\), so the second triangle is also in \(U_C\). Thus there is no interior-edge boundary of \(U_C\).

At a boundary vertex \(v\), the link \(\operatorname{Lk}(v)\) is an interval. The incident triangles occur in a fan

\[
T_1,\dots,T_m,
\]

and consecutive triangles \(T_i,T_{i+1}\) share an edge incident to \(v\). The only boundary edges in the fan are the two extreme edges, so each shared edge \(T_i\cap T_{i+1}\) is non-boundary. Therefore all triangles incident to \(v\) lie in the same component of \(\Lambda_M\). This handles possible fan degeneracies.

It follows that each \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Therefore there is only one component. Hence

\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Running the same open-closed argument upstairs in \(\Sigma\) shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is an infinite covering of a circle, we have

\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Choose a leaf \(\ell_0\) in \(\Sigma\), and traverse the interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\) from \(\ell_0\) to \(G_\beta\ell_0\). Its projection goes once around \(\Lambda_M\).

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). The support line of its image is then an oriented line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line depends continuously on the leaf parameter: inside one triangle this is immediate from affine dependence on the ridge point, and at a side-edge identification both adjacent triangle formulas limit to the same oriented image of the common side edge.

If \(\ell_0\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell_0\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image orientation reverses. Therefore, for the continuous family of oriented support lines \(L_s\), \(s\in[0,1]\), obtained by going once around the quotient leaf circle,

\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

---

## 4. Oriented-line lemma

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and \(m=p\times u\) is the moment vector, with \(p\) any point on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Let \(L_t\), \(t\in[0,1]\), be continuous with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Choose continuous \((u(t),m(t))\), and extend by

\[
(u(t+1),m(t+1))=-(u(t),m(t)).
\]

For \(t\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(\delta\in[0,1]\), define

\[
a(t,\delta)=u(t)\cdot u(t+\delta),
\]

\[
b(t,\delta)=u(t)\cdot m(t+\delta)+u(t+\delta)\cdot m(t).
\]

Then

\[
(a,b)(t,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(t,1)=(-1,0).
\]

Let

\[
I(t,\delta)=(t+\delta,1-\delta).
\]

One checks directly that

\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Normalize to a map

\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}:\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1.
\]

On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\equiv(1,0)\). On the middle circle \(\delta=\frac12\),

\[
\phi(t+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(t,\tfrac12).
\]

Thus the middle-circle map has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map \((1,0)\), so it has degree \(0\), contradiction. Hence some \((t,\delta)\) has

\[
a(t,\delta)=b(t,\delta)=0.
\]

The condition \(a=0\) says the two line directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,m=p\times u)\) and \((v,n=q\times v)\), then

\[
u\cdot n+v\cdot m=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]

When \(u\perp v\), the vanishing of this scalar triple product is exactly the condition that the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. Therefore some two support lines in the family are perpendicular and intersecting.

---

## 5. Displacement and boundary arcs of a leaf

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top. Write its endpoints as

\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]

Its flat length is \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\).

We claim \(|t|<\beta\). If \(t=\beta\), then the top endpoint is \(G_\beta(x,0)\), so the image endpoints coincide, contradicting the cevian lemma. Similarly \(t=-\beta\) is impossible.

If \(t>\beta\), then the segment from \((0,0)\) to \((t,1)\) meets its \(G_\beta\)-translate, the segment from \((t+\beta,0)\) to \((\beta,1)\), in their relative interiors. The intersection occurs at

\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]

Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), hence \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) along the nondegenerate image segment \(f(J)\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Therefore \(|t|<\beta\).

The boundary circle can be parametrized by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). The top endpoint \((x+t,1)\) is equivalent to the bottom point \((x+t-\beta,0)\). Since \(|t|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between the endpoints have flat lengths

\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]

Replacing \(J\) by \(G_\beta J\) changes \(t\) to \(-t\), so for either chosen boundary arc between the endpoints we may choose the lift so that that arc has length \(\beta+t\).

---

## 6. Metric endgame

By the oriented-line lemma, choose two quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\). Let their image segments be \(F(T)\) and \(F(B)\).

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, except possibly for common boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the image segments cannot cross in their relative interiors. Hence at least one of the two image segments does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Rename the leaves so that \(F(B)\) is contained in one closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),

\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line} F(T))=|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]

By the cevian lemma,

\[
|F(B)|>|B|_{\mathrm{flat}}\ge1.
\]

Thus, with

\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line} F(T)),
\]

we have

\[
y>1.
\]

Also \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), since otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), giving \(y=0\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with bottom-to-top horizontal displacement \(t\), chosen so that the boundary arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has flat length

\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]

Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so

\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Let

\[
L=|F(T)|.
\]

By the cevian lemma,

\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

### The \(H\)-inequality

Boundary contraction gives

\[
\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]

The path \(F(H)\) connects the endpoints of \(F(T)\), so

\[
\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]

Equality would force \(F(H)\) to be exactly the straight segment \(F(T)\), which would make interior points of the boundary arc \(H\) map to interior points of \(T\), contradicting embeddedness. Hence

\[
\ell_F(H)>L.
\]

Therefore

\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]

so

\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t.
\]

### The \(D\)-inequality

The path \(F(D)\) connects the two endpoints of \(F(T)\) and passes through \(Q=F(q)\). Hence

\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]

where \(A,C\) are the endpoints of \(F(T)\). If \(|A-C|=L\) and \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then the reflected-triangle estimate gives

\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),

\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]

Boundary contraction gives \(\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\), so

\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]

Thus

\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t.
\]

Combining,

\[
\beta>\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]

Finally, for every real \(t\),

\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]

Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then

\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]

would imply, after squaring,

\[
t<\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]

whereas

\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]

would imply

\[
t>\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]

a contradiction. Equality occurs at \(t=1/\sqrt3\). Therefore

\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

No step of the proposed route fails: the possible fan degeneracies are handled by the boundary-vertex link argument, and the metric argument explicitly allows \(T\) and \(B\) to share one boundary endpoint.

Explain the construction sufficiently, for example via the three-cylinder or triangular-limit construction and the width inequality b≤ℓ/(2sqrt(3))-3πd/4 with d>0 chosen small, and explain why the resulting band has a continuous full-width ruling or bend foliation with bounded horizontal span. Route B: direct PL. Explore resolving the triangular three-triangle model in 3D. $$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points

locally finite collection

Below is a rigorous conditional proof, with the only outside input isolated. If one accepts the standard smooth upper-bound theorem for paper Möbius bands of every aspect ratio \(\mu>\sqrt3\), the original statement follows.

---

## 0. Notation

For \(L,c>0\), set

\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]

on \(\widetilde M_{L,c}=\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). Its quotient is the flat Möbius band of width \(c\) and length \(L\).

A transverse segment in the target strip has endpoints \((X_0,0)\), \((X_1,c)\). I write

\[
\ell_0=\sqrt{(X_1-X_0)^2+c^2}
\]

for its flat target length.

---

## 1. Smooth input used

I use the following standard form of the smooth upper-bound theorem.

**Smooth ruled paper-band theorem.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there is a \(C^2\) embedded isometric paper Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), whose lift

\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]

is \(H_{\mu,1}\)-invariant and quotient-injective, and whose lift has an \(H_{\mu,1}\)-invariant full-width ruling foliation. Equivalently, after parametrizing rulings by their lower endpoint, there is a strictly increasing function \(h\) such that the ruling through \(x\) is the flat segment

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),1)],
\]

and \(E_\mu\) maps \(R_x\) affinely and isometrically to a straight segment in \(\mathbb R^3\).

If one does not want to import this theorem, the proof below should be read as a conditional lemma: this smooth ruled existence statement is exactly the remaining input.

Scaling gives the same statement for any width \(c\) and length \(L=\mu c\).

---

## 2. Clean PL approximation of a smooth ruled band

Fix a smooth ruled target band \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) of length \(L\), width \(c\), aspect \(\mu=L/c\). Let \(H=H_{L,c}\).

Parametrize lifted rulings by lower endpoints:

\[
B_x=(x,0),\qquad T_x=(h(x),c).
\]

The deck map sends the ruling \(R_x=[B_x,T_x]\) to \(R_{\phi(x)}\), where

\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,\qquad h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]

Choose one fundamental interval \(I=[x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), and define

\[
S=\max_{x\in I}|h(x)-x|.
\]

Thus \(S\) bounds the horizontal spans of all rulings, modulo deck transformations.

### Lemma 1: clean secant triangulation

For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), affine on every clean triangle, such that:

1. \(F_0\) descends to an embedded PL Möbius band, hence is quotient-injective.
2. Every boundary edge is mapped to a segment no longer than its target flat length.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

### Proof

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that

\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]

Choose a partition

\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]

so fine that both

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]

for all \(i\). Extend it \(H\)-equivariantly by setting \(x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i)\).

Let

\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]

Consecutive rulings \(R_i=[B_i,T_i]\), \(R_{i+1}=[B_{i+1},T_{i+1}]\) bound the clean quadrilateral

\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i).
\]

Because \(h\) is increasing, these quadrilaterals tile the strip with pairwise disjoint interiors.

Triangulate each \(Q_i\) by a diagonal. Since \(H\) reverses the two boundary lines, the diagonal choice must flip under \(H\). For representatives \(i=0,\dots,N-1\), choose the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). On the \(H\)-image quadrilaterals choose the image diagonal, namely \(B_{i+1+N}T_{i+N}\). Extend periodically by \(H^2\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation.

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by

\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]

and extend affinely on each clean triangle.

Boundary edges are chords of the smooth boundary curve \(E\). Since \(E\) is an isometry, the boundary arc length between two adjacent boundary vertices equals the target flat boundary length, and a chord is no longer than the arc. Hence boundary edges are nonexpanded.

For transverse edges, ruling edges are exact:

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Now consider a forward diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let

\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}},\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Moving one endpoint by \(<\rho\) changes flat distance by \(<\rho\), so \(|d-r|<\rho\). Also,

\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho.
\]

Therefore

\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r-\rho\\
&\ge d-2\rho\\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]

The backward diagonal estimate is identical, using the lower boundary increment instead.

Finally, the horizontal span of a ruling edge is at most \(S<D\), and the horizontal span of a diagonal is at most \(S+\rho<D\).

It remains only to justify embeddedness of the PL secant surface. This is the standard secant approximation theorem for \(C^2\) embedded ruled surfaces. Concretely, writing

\[
\gamma_0(x)=E(B_x),\qquad \gamma_1(x)=E(T_x),
\]

the smooth ruled parametrization is

\[
g(x,s)=(1-s)\gamma_0(x)+s\gamma_1(x).
\]

Developability gives that the tangent plane is constant along each ruling, so the planes of the two secant triangles between \(R_i\) and \(R_{i+1}\) converge uniformly to the smooth tangent planes as the partition is refined. Thus, after refining the partition further, the PL surface is \(C^0\)-close and facetwise tangent-plane-close to the embedded smooth surface. A tubular-neighborhood projection then restricts to a degree-one local homeomorphism from the PL surface to the smooth band, hence to a homeomorphism. Therefore the PL surface is embedded. ∎

---

## 3. Pullback criterion

Now suppose we have a target PL band \(F_0\) in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) of length \(L\), width \(c\), with deck map \(H_{L,c}\), satisfying:

- boundary edges are nonexpanded;
- transverse edges satisfy
  \[
  |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon;
  \]
- transverse horizontal spans are \(\le D\);
- \(F_0\) is quotient-injective.

Let \(a<1\), \(L=a\beta\), and define

\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]

Then

\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]

So \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Define

\[
f=F_0\circ A.
\]

Let \(\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0)\). Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every clean triangle.

Boundary edges are strictly shortened: if a boundary edge in \(\Sigma\) has length \(\Delta x\), then its target length is \(a\Delta x\), so

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le a\Delta x<\Delta x.
\]

For a transverse edge, let its target horizontal span be \(t\le D\). Its target flat length is

\[
\sqrt{t^2+c^2},
\]

while its original length in \(\Sigma\) is

\[
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}.
\]

Thus it is enough to choose parameters so that

\[
\varepsilon
<
\min_{0\le t\le D}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right).
\]

Then every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Quotient-injectivity also pulls back:

\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)
\iff
Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q).
\]

---

## 4. Choosing the constants

Let \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose

\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]

Use the smooth theorem for the normalized width-one band of aspect \(\mu\). Let \(S_\mu\) be the corresponding ruling-span bound, and set

\[
D_\mu=S_\mu+1.
\]

Temporarily take \(a=1\) and put

\[
c_*=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]

Then

\[
m_*=
\min_{0\le t\le c_*D_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c_*^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2+1}
\right)
>0.
\]

Now choose \(a<1\), with \(a\) sufficiently close to \(1\) and \(a>\mu/\beta\). Put

\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu},
\qquad
L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]

Then \(c>1\), and by continuity,

\[
m=
\min_{0\le t\le cD_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right)
>0.
\]

Apply Lemma 1 to the scaled smooth target band of width \(c\), length \(L\), with

\[
D=cD_\mu,
\qquad
\varepsilon<m.
\]

The resulting \(F_0\) satisfies all target properties. Pulling back by \(A(x,y)=(ax,cy)\) gives the required \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and the desired PL map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized in the exact category stated in the problem.

## Short answer

Route A works unconditionally. The prior “smooth ruled band” black box can be replaced by Sadowsky’s explicit three-cylinder construction, in the slightly weaker \(C^1\), piecewise-\(C^\infty\) developable category; that is already enough for the clean-PL secant-and-pullback argument. If one insists on a \(C^\infty\) source theorem, this is the classical Sadowsky/Halpern–Weaver smoothing theorem, but the PL proof below avoids any hidden smooth/PL mismatch.

---

## Route A: Sadowsky input and clean-PL realization

### Classical input used

Use the following form of Sadowsky’s construction.

**Sadowsky three-cylinder theorem, sufficient form.**  
Let \(l>0\), \(b>0\), and choose \(d>0\) so that

\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Then the flat rectangle of length \(l\) and width \(2b\), with opposite short sides identified with a half-twist, has an embedded isometric developable Möbius realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). The realization is \(C^1\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), made from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces, and has a continuous full-width ruling foliation by straight generators.

This is exactly Sadowsky’s 1930 three-rod construction: in the Hinz–Fried translation, the rods have diameters \(d,d,2d\), the surface consists of planar and semicylindrical sections, the midline length is \(l=3L\), and the maximal half-width satisfies

\[
b\le \frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

The strict inequality gives positive clearance, so there is no boundary contact/overlap. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

Now fix \(\mu>\sqrt3\). Set \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\). Choose

\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]

Then

\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Thus Sadowsky gives an embedded isometric Möbius band of width \(1\), length \(\mu\), with a continuous full-width ruling foliation. Its lift

\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]

is invariant under

\[
H_{\mu,1}(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y)
\]

and quotient-injective. The full-width rulings lift to segments

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),1)]
\]

with \(h\) continuous and strictly increasing. On one fundamental interval, \(|h(x)-x|\) is bounded.

For comparison, Halpern–Weaver is the classical smooth source for the smooth paper-band version, and Schwartz’s modern account uses the standard definition of a smooth embedded paper Möbius band and recalls the continuous bend foliation for smooth embedded paper bands. ([ams.org](https://www.ams.org/journals/tran/1977-230-00/S0002-9947-1977-0474388-1/home.html?utm_source=openai)) But the proof below only needs the \(C^1\), piecewise-smooth Sadowsky band, so there is no category gap.

---

## PL secant lemma, in the needed \(C^1\) form

Let

\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]

on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). Suppose \(E\) is a quotient-injective embedded isometric developable band of width \(c\), length \(L\), invariant under \(H_{L,c}\), with continuous full-width rulings

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]

Let

\[
S=\sup |h(x)-x|
\]

over one fundamental interval. Then for every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), affine on each triangle, such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective.
2. Boundary edges are nonexpanded.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

The proof is the same secant proof as in the prior t2 draft: choose adjacent rulings \(R_i=[B_i,T_i]\) so close that both bottom and top endpoint increments are \(<\rho\), triangulate each clean quadrilateral between \(R_i\) and \(R_{i+1}\), and put \(F_0\) on vertices equal to \(E\). Ruling edges are exact; diagonal edges lose at most \(2\rho\) by the triangle inequality; boundary chords are no longer than their boundary arcs. Embeddedness follows from the standard inscribed-PL approximation theorem for compact \(C^1\) embedded surfaces: for a sufficiently fine partition, the secant surface lies in a tubular neighborhood and projects homeomorphically to the original band. The Sadowsky seams cause no problem because the surface is \(C^1\), and one may include seam rulings among the partition rulings.

---

## Pullback to the required clean category

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose

\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]

Use the Sadowsky band of normalized aspect \(\mu\), and let \(D_\mu>S_\mu\) be a ruling-span bound plus margin. Choose \(a<1\), close to \(1\), with

\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta},
\qquad
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}>1,
\qquad
L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\), so the target has width \(c\) and length \(L\).

Define

\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]

Then

\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]

So \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Now choose \(\varepsilon>0\) small enough that

\[
\varepsilon<
\min_{0\le t\le cD_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right).
\]

This minimum is positive because at \(a=1\) we have \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and positivity persists for \(a\) close enough to \(1\).

Apply the PL secant lemma to get \(F_0\), and define

\[
f=F_0\circ A.
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]

Then \(\mathcal T\) is a locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

### Boundary contraction

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(A(p)A(q)\) has flat length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le a|p-q|<|p-q|.
\]

So the boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Transverse expansion

If \(pq\) is a transverse edge, let \(t\le cD_\mu\) be its target horizontal span after applying \(A\). Then

\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2+c^2},
\]

while

\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}.
\]

By the choice of \(\varepsilon\),

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]

Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

### Quotient-injectivity

Because \(F_0\) is quotient-injective and \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\iff Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore \(f\) realizes \(\beta\). Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\) was arbitrary, every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## Route B: direct triangular PL model

Let

\[
u=\frac{\beta}{3},
\]

and consider the clean triangle with vertices

\[
(0,0),\qquad (2u,0),\qquad (u,1).
\]

Its boundary edge has length

\[
\ell=2u=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\]

and its two transverse edges have length

\[
r=\sqrt{1+u^2}=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]

Indeed,

\[
r<\ell
\iff
1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}<\frac{4\beta^2}{9}
\iff
\beta^2>3.
\]

So for \(\beta>\sqrt3\), one can choose \(s\) with

\[
r<s<\ell,
\]

and a single triangle could be affinely mapped to an equilateral triangle of side \(s\), giving the desired edge inequalities.

The obstruction is global injectivity. The natural three-triangle quotient has only three vertex orbits. Write

\[
B_n=(2nu,0),\qquad T_n=((2n+1)u,1).
\]

Then \(G_\beta(B_n)=T_{n+1}\) and \(G_\beta(T_n)=B_{n+2}\), so the quotient has three vertex classes. The three fundamental triangles may be taken as

\[
(B_0,B_1,T_0),\qquad (T_0,T_1,B_1),\qquad (B_1,B_2,T_1).
\]

Any map using only those three vertex images sends every edge with the same pair of endpoint classes to the same straight segment. But in this complex, boundary edges and transverse glued edges share the same endpoint pairs. Hence a boundary edge and an interior transverse edge necessarily overlap in the target. Therefore the raw three-triangle triangular model cannot be quotient-injective, no matter how the three target vertices are perturbed in \(\mathbb R^3\).

A subdivision can in principle separate these coincident edges, but then one must solve a real clearance problem near the three triangular vertices. The Sadowsky/secant construction above is exactly a controlled subdivision-and-perturbation of that triangular limit: the parameter \(d>0\) gives positive clearance, and the strict inequality \(\beta>\sqrt3\) supplies the edge-length margin.

---

Below is a rigorous conditional proof, with the only outside input isolated. If one accepts the standard smooth upper-bound theorem for paper Möbius bands of every aspect ratio \(\mu>\sqrt3\), the original statement follows.

---

## 0. Notation

For \(L,c>0\), set

\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]

on \(\widetilde M_{L,c}=\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). Its quotient is the flat Möbius band of width \(c\) and length \(L\).

A transverse segment in the target strip has endpoints \((X_0,0)\), \((X_1,c)\). I write

\[
\ell_0=\sqrt{(X_1-X_0)^2+c^2}
\]

for its flat target length.

---

## 1. Smooth input used

I use the following standard form of the smooth upper-bound theorem.

**Smooth ruled paper-band theorem.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there is a \(C^2\) embedded isometric paper Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), whose lift

\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]

is \(H_{\mu,1}\)-invariant and quotient-injective, and whose lift has an \(H_{\mu,1}\)-invariant full-width ruling foliation. Equivalently, after parametrizing rulings by their lower endpoint, there is a strictly increasing function \(h\) such that the ruling through \(x\) is the flat segment

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),1)],
\]

and \(E_\mu\) maps \(R_x\) affinely and isometrically to a straight segment in \(\mathbb R^3\).

If one does not want to import this theorem, the proof below should be read as a conditional lemma: this smooth ruled existence statement is exactly the remaining input.

Scaling gives the same statement for any width \(c\) and length \(L=\mu c\).

---

## 2. Clean PL approximation of a smooth ruled band

Fix a smooth ruled target band \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) of length \(L\), width \(c\), aspect \(\mu=L/c\). Let \(H=H_{L,c}\).

Parametrize lifted rulings by lower endpoints:

\[
B_x=(x,0),\qquad T_x=(h(x),c).
\]

The deck map sends the ruling \(R_x=[B_x,T_x]\) to \(R_{\phi(x)}\), where

\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,\qquad h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]

Choose one fundamental interval \(I=[x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), and define

\[
S=\max_{x\in I}|h(x)-x|.
\]

Thus \(S\) bounds the horizontal spans of all rulings, modulo deck transformations.

### Lemma 1: clean secant triangulation

For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), affine on every clean triangle, such that:

1. \(F_0\) descends to an embedded PL Möbius band, hence is quotient-injective.
2. Every boundary edge is mapped to a segment no longer than its target flat length.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

### Proof

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that

\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]

Choose a partition

\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]

so fine that both

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]

for all \(i\). Extend it \(H\)-equivariantly by setting \(x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i)\).

Let

\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]

Consecutive rulings \(R_i=[B_i,T_i]\), \(R_{i+1}=[B_{i+1},T_{i+1}]\) bound the clean quadrilateral

\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i).
\]

Because \(h\) is increasing, these quadrilaterals tile the strip with pairwise disjoint interiors.

Triangulate each \(Q_i\) by a diagonal. Since \(H\) reverses the two boundary lines, the diagonal choice must flip under \(H\). For representatives \(i=0,\dots,N-1\), choose the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). On the \(H\)-image quadrilaterals choose the image diagonal, namely \(B_{i+1+N}T_{i+N}\). Extend periodically by \(H^2\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation.

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by

\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]

and extend affinely on each clean triangle.

Boundary edges are chords of the smooth boundary curve \(E\). Since \(E\) is an isometry, the boundary arc length between two adjacent boundary vertices equals the target flat boundary length, and a chord is no longer than the arc. Hence boundary edges are nonexpanded.

For transverse edges, ruling edges are exact:

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Now consider a forward diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let

\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}},\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Moving one endpoint by \(<\rho\) changes flat distance by \(<\rho\), so \(|d-r|<\rho\). Also,

\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho.
\]

Therefore

\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r-\rho\\
&\ge d-2\rho\\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]

The backward diagonal estimate is identical, using the lower boundary increment instead.

Finally, the horizontal span of a ruling edge is at most \(S<D\), and the horizontal span of a diagonal is at most \(S+\rho<D\).

It remains only to justify embeddedness of the PL secant surface. This is the standard secant approximation theorem for \(C^2\) embedded ruled surfaces. Concretely, writing

\[
\gamma_0(x)=E(B_x),\qquad \gamma_1(x)=E(T_x),
\]

the smooth ruled parametrization is

\[
g(x,s)=(1-s)\gamma_0(x)+s\gamma_1(x).
\]

Developability gives that the tangent plane is constant along each ruling, so the planes of the two secant triangles between \(R_i\) and \(R_{i+1}\) converge uniformly to the smooth tangent planes as the partition is refined. Thus, after refining the partition further, the PL surface is \(C^0\)-close and facetwise tangent-plane-close to the embedded smooth surface. A tubular-neighborhood projection then restricts to a degree-one local homeomorphism from the PL surface to the smooth band, hence to a homeomorphism. Therefore the PL surface is embedded. ∎

---

## 3. Pullback criterion

Now suppose we have a target PL band \(F_0\) in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) of length \(L\), width \(c\), with deck map \(H_{L,c}\), satisfying:

- boundary edges are nonexpanded;
- transverse edges satisfy
  \[
  |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon;
  \]
- transverse horizontal spans are \(\le D\);
- \(F_0\) is quotient-injective.

Let \(a<1\), \(L=a\beta\), and define

\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]

Then

\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]

So \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Define

\[
f=F_0\circ A.
\]

Let \(\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0)\). Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every clean triangle.

Boundary edges are strictly shortened: if a boundary edge in \(\Sigma\) has length \(\Delta x\), then its target length is \(a\Delta x\), so

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le a\Delta x<\Delta x.
\]

For a transverse edge, let its target horizontal span be \(t\le D\). Its target flat length is

\[
\sqrt{t^2+c^2},
\]

while its original length in \(\Sigma\) is

\[
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}.
\]

Thus it is enough to choose parameters so that

\[
\varepsilon
<
\min_{0\le t\le D}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right).
\]

Then every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Quotient-injectivity also pulls back:

\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)
\iff
Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q).
\]

---

## 4. Choosing the constants

Let \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose

\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]

Use the smooth theorem for the normalized width-one band of aspect \(\mu\). Let \(S_\mu\) be the corresponding ruling-span bound, and set

\[
D_\mu=S_\mu+1.
\]

Temporarily take \(a=1\) and put

\[
c_*=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]

Then

\[
m_*=
\min_{0\le t\le c_*D_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c_*^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2+1}
\right)
>0.
\]

Now choose \(a<1\), with \(a\) sufficiently close to \(1\) and \(a>\mu/\beta\). Put

\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu},
\qquad
L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]

Then \(c>1\), and by continuity,

\[
m=
\min_{0\le t\le cD_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right)
>0.
\]

Apply Lemma 1 to the scaled smooth target band of width \(c\), length \(L\), with

\[
D=cD_\mu,
\qquad
\varepsilon<m.
\]

The resulting \(F_0\) satisfies all target properties. Pulling back by \(A(x,y)=(ax,cy)\) gives the required \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and the desired PL map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized in the exact category stated in the problem.

Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let

$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I find the proposed proof **essentially sound**, but two points should be stated more carefully:

1. The quotient of the triangulation is safest to call a **finite regular \(\Delta\)-complex / PL cellulation**; if a literal simplicial complex is desired, take its barycentric subdivision.
2. In the metric endgame, the \(H\)-arc strictness does **not** need a delicate equality-case argument; strict boundary contraction already gives the strict inequality.

Below is a polished repaired proof.

---

## Theorem

If \(\beta>0\) is realized, then  
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]

### 1. Quotient and PL embedding

Let \(\mathcal T\) be the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The realization condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous injective map
\[
F:M\to \mathbb R^3.
\]
Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\ne0\), every point is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were not injective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f|_\Delta\) is injective on every triangle.

Therefore no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\); injectivity on \(\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, so there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of triangles. The quotient is therefore a finite regular PL \(\Delta\)-complex structure on \(M\). No triangle has self-identifications. If one wants an honest simplicial triangulation, barycentrically subdivide this finite \(\Delta\)-complex. The map \(F\) is PL, affine and injective on each original triangle.

---

### 2. Squeeze-cevian lemma

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Thus every cevian from \(a\) to \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened:
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]

Consequences:

- every leaf segment has nonzero image length;
- every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened;
- every boundary edge is strictly shortened;
- since \(F\) is affine on each boundary edge, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in path length.

---

### 3. Leaf graph and oriented support lines

For each triangle, take the interval of its apex-to-boundary cevians. The two endpoints correspond to the two nonboundary side edges. Glue endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary side edges are identified. This gives a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\) in the quotient. Similarly one has an upstairs graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

Each nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so each vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus each component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We show \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of all triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). Across any nonboundary edge, the adjacent triangle belongs to the same component of \(\Lambda_M\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan
\[
T_1,\dots,T_m,
\]
and consecutive triangles share nonboundary edges, except for the two extreme boundary edges. Hence all triangles incident to that boundary vertex lie in the same component. Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a connected covering of a circle. It is not a finite circle cover: no nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) fixes a leaf, because comparing the bottom and top endpoints of a lifted leaf gives an immediate contradiction. Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong \mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This line depends continuously on the leaf parameter: inside a triangle this is affine dependence on the ridge point, and across a shared nonboundary edge the two limiting oriented image segments agree.

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment but with opposite orientation. Therefore, along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\), the oriented support line path satisfies
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

---

### 4. Oriented-line lemma

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines with
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]
Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\), and extend by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]
satisfies
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

If \((a,b)\) never vanished, then normalizing would give a map
\[
\phi:\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1.
\]
On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. But on the middle circle \(\delta=\tfrac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12),
\]
so the middle-circle map has odd degree. This contradicts its homotopy through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map. Hence some pair satisfies
\[
a(s,\delta)=b(s,\delta)=0.
\]

The condition \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the condition \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. Therefore the support-line family contains two perpendicular intersecting support lines.

---

### 5. Leaf displacement and boundary arcs

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top, with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

We claim
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]
If \(t=\beta\), the two endpoints are identified by \(G_\beta\), so their images under \(f\) coincide, contradicting the positive image length of \(J\). The case \(t=-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\).

If \(t>\beta\), then \(J\) intersects \(G_\beta J\) in relative interiors. After translating \(x\) to \(0\), the two segments are
\[
(0,0)\to(t,1)
\]
and
\[
(t+\beta,0)\to(\beta,1),
\]
which meet at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate image segment \(f(J)\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Therefore \(|t|<\beta\).

Now parametrize the quotient boundary by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). The bottom point \((x,0)\) has coordinate \(x\), while the top point \((x+t,1)\) corresponds to bottom coordinate
\[
x+t-\beta.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between the endpoints have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
Replacing the lift \(J\) by \(G_\beta J\) changes \(t\) to \(-t\), so for either chosen boundary arc one may choose the lift so that that arc has length \(\beta+t\).

---

### 6. Metric endgame

By the oriented-line lemma, choose two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Thus at least one of the two image segments lies in a closed ray from \(P\). Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and let
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the cevian lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus, writing
\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T)),
\]
we have
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), forcing \(y=0\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1),
\]
and choose the lift so that the boundary arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Let
\[
L=|F(T)|.
\]
By the cevian lemma,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), boundary contraction gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects the endpoints of \(F(T)\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects the endpoints \(A,C\) of \(F(T)\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(|A-C|=L\) and \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary contraction gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
hence
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.

non-collinear points

locally finite collection

$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the

## Short answer

Route A works unconditionally. The prior “smooth ruled band” black box can be replaced by Sadowsky’s explicit three-cylinder construction, in the slightly weaker \(C^1\), piecewise-\(C^\infty\) developable category; that is already enough for the clean-PL secant-and-pullback argument. If one insists on a \(C^\infty\) source theorem, this is the classical Sadowsky/Halpern–Weaver smoothing theorem, but the PL proof below avoids any hidden smooth/PL mismatch.

---

## Route A: Sadowsky input and clean-PL realization

### Classical input used

Use the following form of Sadowsky’s construction.

**Sadowsky three-cylinder theorem, sufficient form.**  
Let \(l>0\), \(b>0\), and choose \(d>0\) so that

\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Then the flat rectangle of length \(l\) and width \(2b\), with opposite short sides identified with a half-twist, has an embedded isometric developable Möbius realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). The realization is \(C^1\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), made from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces, and has a continuous full-width ruling foliation by straight generators.

This is exactly Sadowsky’s 1930 three-rod construction: in the Hinz–Fried translation, the rods have diameters \(d,d,2d\), the surface consists of planar and semicylindrical sections, the midline length is \(l=3L\), and the maximal half-width satisfies

\[
b\le \frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

The strict inequality gives positive clearance, so there is no boundary contact/overlap. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

Now fix \(\mu>\sqrt3\). Set \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\). Choose

\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]

Then

\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Thus Sadowsky gives an embedded isometric Möbius band of width \(1\), length \(\mu\), with a continuous full-width ruling foliation. Its lift

\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]

is invariant under

\[
H_{\mu,1}(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y)
\]

and quotient-injective. The full-width rulings lift to segments

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),1)]
\]

with \(h\) continuous and strictly increasing. On one fundamental interval, \(|h(x)-x|\) is bounded.

For comparison, Halpern–Weaver is the classical smooth source for the smooth paper-band version, and Schwartz’s modern account uses the standard definition of a smooth embedded paper Möbius band and recalls the continuous bend foliation for smooth embedded paper bands. ([ams.org](https://www.ams.org/journals/tran/1977-230-00/S0002-9947-1977-0474388-1/home.html?utm_source=openai)) But the proof below only needs the \(C^1\), piecewise-smooth Sadowsky band, so there is no category gap.

---

## PL secant lemma, in the needed \(C^1\) form

Let

\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]

on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). Suppose \(E\) is a quotient-injective embedded isometric developable band of width \(c\), length \(L\), invariant under \(H_{L,c}\), with continuous full-width rulings

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]

Let

\[
S=\sup |h(x)-x|
\]

over one fundamental interval. Then for every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), affine on each triangle, such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective.
2. Boundary edges are nonexpanded.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

The proof is the same secant proof as in the prior t2 draft: choose adjacent rulings \(R_i=[B_i,T_i]\) so close that both bottom and top endpoint increments are \(<\rho\), triangulate each clean quadrilateral between \(R_i\) and \(R_{i+1}\), and put \(F_0\) on vertices equal to \(E\). Ruling edges are exact; diagonal edges lose at most \(2\rho\) by the triangle inequality; boundary chords are no longer than their boundary arcs. Embeddedness follows from the standard inscribed-PL approximation theorem for compact \(C^1\) embedded surfaces: for a sufficiently fine partition, the secant surface lies in a tubular neighborhood and projects homeomorphically to the original band. The Sadowsky seams cause no problem because the surface is \(C^1\), and one may include seam rulings among the partition rulings.

---

## Pullback to the required clean category

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose

\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]

Use the Sadowsky band of normalized aspect \(\mu\), and let \(D_\mu>S_\mu\) be a ruling-span bound plus margin. Choose \(a<1\), close to \(1\), with

\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta},
\qquad
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}>1,
\qquad
L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\), so the target has width \(c\) and length \(L\).

Define

\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]

Then

\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]

So \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Now choose \(\varepsilon>0\) small enough that

\[
\varepsilon<
\min_{0\le t\le cD_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right).
\]

This minimum is positive because at \(a=1\) we have \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and positivity persists for \(a\) close enough to \(1\).

Apply the PL secant lemma to get \(F_0\), and define

\[
f=F_0\circ A.
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]

Then \(\mathcal T\) is a locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

### Boundary contraction

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(A(p)A(q)\) has flat length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le a|p-q|<|p-q|.
\]

So the boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Transverse expansion

If \(pq\) is a transverse edge, let \(t\le cD_\mu\) be its target horizontal span after applying \(A\). Then

\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2+c^2},
\]

while

\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}.
\]

By the choice of \(\varepsilon\),

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]

Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

### Quotient-injectivity

Because \(F_0\) is quotient-injective and \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\iff Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore \(f\) realizes \(\beta\). Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\) was arbitrary, every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## Route B: direct triangular PL model

Let

\[
u=\frac{\beta}{3},
\]

and consider the clean triangle with vertices

\[
(0,0),\qquad (2u,0),\qquad (u,1).
\]

Its boundary edge has length

\[
\ell=2u=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\]

and its two transverse edges have length

\[
r=\sqrt{1+u^2}=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]

Indeed,

\[
r<\ell
\iff
1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}<\frac{4\beta^2}{9}
\iff
\beta^2>3.
\]

So for \(\beta>\sqrt3\), one can choose \(s\) with

\[
r<s<\ell,
\]

and a single triangle could be affinely mapped to an equilateral triangle of side \(s\), giving the desired edge inequalities.

The obstruction is global injectivity. The natural three-triangle quotient has only three vertex orbits. Write

\[
B_n=(2nu,0),\qquad T_n=((2n+1)u,1).
\]

Then \(G_\beta(B_n)=T_{n+1}\) and \(G_\beta(T_n)=B_{n+2}\), so the quotient has three vertex classes. The three fundamental triangles may be taken as

\[
(B_0,B_1,T_0),\qquad (T_0,T_1,B_1),\qquad (B_1,B_2,T_1).
\]

Any map using only those three vertex images sends every edge with the same pair of endpoint classes to the same straight segment. But in this complex, boundary edges and transverse glued edges share the same endpoint pairs. Hence a boundary edge and an interior transverse edge necessarily overlap in the target. Therefore the raw three-triangle triangular model cannot be quotient-injective, no matter how the three target vertices are perturbed in \(\mathbb R^3\).

A subdivision can in principle separate these coincident edges, but then one must solve a real clearance problem near the three triangular vertices. The Sadowsky/secant construction above is exactly a controlled subdivision-and-perturbation of that triangular limit: the parameter \(d>0\) gives positive clearance, and the strict inequality \(\beta>\sqrt3\) supplies the edge-length margin.

---

A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of partial Sigma; a clean triangulation is a countable locally finite collection of clean triangles with disjoint interiors and union Sigma; it is G_beta-invariant if G_beta preserves it. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into R^3 that strictly decreases the boundary edge contained in partial Sigma and strictly increases the other two edges. A number beta is realized if there is a continuous f: Sigma -> R^3 that is a squeeze map on every triangle of some G_beta-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies f(p)=f(q) iff p=G_beta^k(q) for some integer k. Prove that the realized set is exactly (sqrt(3), infinity), hence its infimum is sqrt(3). Structure the proof as follows. First prove the lower bound beta realized implies beta > sqrt(3): descend f to an embedded PL Moebius band; prove the finite quotient cellulation; prove the squeeze-cevian lemma; define the apex-to-boundary leaf graph and prove it is a single circle, with lifted leaf space a line; prove the oriented-line lemma using direction-moment coordinates and the odd-degree/Borsuk-Ulam argument; obtain two perpendicular intersecting support lines; then give the boundary-arc metric argument yielding beta > max(sqrt(1+t^2)+t, sqrt(5+t^2)-t) >= sqrt(3). Handle explicitly the possibility that selected leaves share a boundary endpoint or that the line intersection is outside one or both image segments. Second prove the upper bound: state a Sadowsky three-cylinder construction lemma giving, for each mu > sqrt(3), an embedded width-one length-mu developable Moebius band with continuous full-width rulings; include enough explanation of the construction and width inequality to make the lemma credible. Then prove a PL secant approximation lemma for a compact C1 ruled embedded band: for sufficiently fine invariant clean triangulations, the secant PL map is quotient-injective, boundary edges are nonexpanded, and transverse edges lose at most epsilon in length, with a uniform horizontal-span bound. Finally choose sqrt(3) < mu < beta, choose a < 1 close to 1 with c = a beta / mu > 1, pull back the scaled Sadowsky band by A(x,y) = (a x, c y), and check strict boundary contraction, strict transverse expansion, G_beta-invariance, and the iff quotient-injectivity condition. Write the proof in a polished theorem-lemma style suitable for an independent referee.
