## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers, let \(\mathbb R\) be the real numbers, let
\[
\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1],
\qquad
\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\},
\]
and, for \(\beta>0\), let
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the usual \(k\)-fold iterate, using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) for negative \(k\), and the identity for \(k=0\). A clean triangle is the convex hull in \(\Sigma\) of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) is a countable locally finite family of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors whose union is \(\Sigma\). A clean triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) which strictly shortens the unique edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there are a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) such that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\), and
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\).

We prove exactly that the set of realized \(\beta\)’s is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\). In particular, its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is not a relaxation of the stated problem.

## Proof

We write \(|\cdot|\) for Euclidean length in the flat strip or in \(\mathbb R^3\), according to context. The proof has two parts.

First, we prove the lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Second, we prove the upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

# Part I. The lower bound

Assume throughout Part I that \(\beta>0\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

## 1. Cevian lengthening and face-to-face refinement

Let \(\Delta\) be a clean triangle. Since its vertices lie on the two boundary components of \(\Sigma\), exactly two of its vertices lie on one boundary component and form the unique boundary edge. Write
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\},
\]
where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\in\partial\Sigma\) is the opposite vertex.

Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(0\le s\le1\), set
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1
\]
and define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
Because \(A\) strictly lengthens the two nonboundary edges,
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Because \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\left(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\right).
\]
The boundary edge is strictly shortened, so \(q''(s)<0\). Hence \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0
\]
for every \(s\in[0,1]\). Thus every cevian from \(a\) to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened:
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]

We now make the triangulation face-to-face. In a clean triangulation, nonboundary edges are already face-to-face: two nonboundary clean edges either are disjoint, meet only in boundary endpoints, or coincide. Indeed, if two full-width nonboundary edges overlap in a nontrivial segment, they lie on the same line and hence coincide; if they crossed transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\), the local planar tiling near the crossing would force interiors of triangles to overlap. The only possible non-face-to-face phenomenon is that boundary vertices subdivide boundary edges.

For each triangle \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), list all boundary vertices of the triangulation lying on the boundary segment \([r_0,r_1]\):
\[
r_0=s_0,s_1,\dots,s_m=r_1
\]
in order. Local finiteness implies this list is finite. Replace \(\Delta\) by the clean triangles
\[
\operatorname{conv}\{a,s_i,s_{i+1}\},\qquad i=0,\dots,m-1.
\]
Doing this equivariantly for all triangles gives a \(G_\beta\)-invariant face-to-face clean refinement. The same map \(f\) is still affine on each refined triangle. The new boundary edges are subsegments of old boundary edges, hence are strictly shortened. The new nonboundary edges are cevians of old triangles, hence are strictly lengthened by the cevian lemma. Thus, after this harmless refinement, we may assume from now on that \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

Two immediate consequences will be used repeatedly:

1. every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in image path length;
2. every leaf segment, meaning every cevian in a triangle, has nonzero image length and is strictly lengthened.

---

## 2. The quotient is a finite PL embedded Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is a single circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The orbit condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\). Therefore \(f\) descends to a map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The same orbit condition says exactly that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We next record that the quotient cellulation is finite and PL. For \(k\ne0\), the displacement of \(G_\beta^k\) is at least \(\beta\): even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while odd powers change \(x\) by an odd multiple of \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were non-injective on a triangle \(\Delta\), then, because \(f|_\Delta\) is affine, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f|_\Delta\) is injective on every triangle.

Therefore no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting the fact that \(G_\beta^k\) has no fixed point for \(k\ne0\).

A compact fundamental domain meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness. Thus there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of triangles, and the quotient is a finite regular PL cellulation of the Möbius band. The descended map \(F\) is affine and injective on each quotient triangle.

---

## 3. The clean leaf space is one circle

For each clean triangle \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), consider the interval of cevians
\[
[a,r(s)],\qquad 0\le s\le1.
\]
The two endpoints \(s=0,1\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\Delta\). Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary edges are identified in the face-to-face quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), called the leaf graph.

Each nonboundary edge of a surface triangulation is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence every connected component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We show \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all quotient triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). If a triangle in \(U_C\) meets another triangle across a nonboundary edge, then the corresponding intervals in \(\Lambda_M\) share an endpoint, so the adjacent triangle is also in \(U_C\).

At a boundary vertex \(v\), the incident triangles form a fan
\[
T_1,\dots,T_m.
\]
Consecutive triangles in this fan share nonboundary edges, except for the two extreme boundary edges. Thus all triangles incident to \(v\) lie in the same component of \(\Lambda_M\). It follows that \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is connected:
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs in \(\Sigma\) shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a connected covering of a circle. It cannot be a finite circle cover, because no nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
An even nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), and an odd power swaps the boundary components; equality of the same unordered segment would force simultaneously \(t=(2m+1)\beta\) and \(t=-(2m+1)\beta\), impossible. Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image is a nondegenerate straight segment, so it has an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously with the leaf parameter. Inside one triangle this is immediate from affine dependence on the cevian parameter; at a shared nonboundary edge the two limiting segments agree with the same bottom-to-top orientation.

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\), oriented from bottom to top, has endpoints \(G_\beta t\) and \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the geometric image segment is the same but with opposite orientation. Therefore, as one moves along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\), the path of oriented support lines satisfies
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

---

## 4. Oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

We need the following elementary lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]
Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. The vector \(m\) is independent of the choice of \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Let
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta).
\]
Using the antiperiodicity above,
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanished. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
would define a continuous map from the cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1]\) to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map with this symmetry has odd degree: lifting its argument \(\theta(s)\), one has
\[
\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi n
\]
for some integer \(n\), and therefore the total degree is \(1+2n\). But the middle circle is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant bottom circle, so its degree must be \(0\), a contradiction. Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere. This zero cannot occur at \(\delta=0\) or \(\delta=1\).

Now take the two lines corresponding to \(s\) and \(s+\delta\). The equation \(a=0\) says their directions \(u,v\) are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by
\[
(u,p\times u),\qquad (v,q\times v),
\]
then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
For nonparallel lines, the vanishing of this scalar triple product is exactly the condition that the two lines are coplanar. Since here the directions are perpendicular, the two lines are nonparallel; coplanar nonparallel lines intersect. Thus the family contains two distinct perpendicular intersecting lines. \(\square\)

Applying this lemma to the oriented support-line path obtained from the leaf circle, we get two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

---

## 5. Leaf displacement and boundary arc lengths

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top, with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}.
\]

We claim
\[
|s|<\beta.
\]
If \(s=\beta\), then the top endpoint is \(G_\beta(x,0)\), so the two endpoints have the same image under \(f\), contradicting the fact that the image of \(J\) is a nondegenerate segment. The case \(s=-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\).

If \(s>\beta\), translate horizontally so the leaf is the segment from \((0,0)\) to \((s,1)\). Its \(G_\beta\)-translate is the segment from
\[
(s+\beta,0)
\quad\text{to}\quad
(\beta,1).
\]
These two segments meet in their relative interiors at height
\[
y=\frac{s+\beta}{2s}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the segment \(J\). The case \(s<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Therefore \(|s|<\beta\).

The quotient boundary is a circle of length \(2\beta\). Parametrize it by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). The top point \((x+s,1)\) is equivalent to the bottom point with coordinate \(x+s-\beta\) modulo \(2\beta\). Since \(|s|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of the quotient leaf have flat lengths
\[
\beta-s,\qquad \beta+s.
\]

For the metric argument below, it is best to define the parameter \(t\) through boundary arc lengths, not by choosing a preferred lift. Given a quotient leaf \(T\) and one of the two boundary arcs \(D\) between its endpoints, define \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\) by
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\qquad
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(H\) is the other boundary arc and \(\ell_0\) denotes flat boundary length. For some lift of \(T\), \(t\) is equal to the horizontal displacement \(s\) or \(-s\). Hence the flat length of \(T\) is
\[
|T|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

---

## 6. Metric endgame

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two distinct quotient leaves whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Write \(F(T)\) and \(F(B)\) for the image segments.

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the Möbius band; they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Therefore at least one of the two image segments is contained in a closed ray from \(P\) along its support line. Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The cevian lemma gives
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because if it were, then \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), giving \(y=0\). This handles the possible shared-endpoint cases: the other endpoint of \(B\) may coincide with an endpoint of \(T\), but \(q\) does not.

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Define \(t\) by
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\qquad
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
By the cevian lemma,
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

### The \(H\)-inequality

Boundary contraction gives
\[
\ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_F\) denotes image path length. Since \(F(H)\) is a path from \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t.}
\]

### The \(D\)-inequality

The path \(F(D)\) goes from \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(|A-C|=L\) and \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) orthogonally to the line \(AC\), the left side is minimized when the projection lies at the midpoint of \(AC\).

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary contraction gives
\[
\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\boxed{\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t.}
\]

Combining the two boxed inequalities,
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Therefore every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# Part II. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

The construction uses a classical explicit developable Möbius band due to Sadowsky. We state the precise form needed, with the relevant normalization.

## 7. Sadowsky three-cylinder lemma

**Sadowsky three-cylinder lemma.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric Möbius band
\[
I_\mu:M_{\mu,1}\to\mathbb R^3,
\]
where
\[
M_{\mu,1}=([0,\mu]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\mu,1-y),
\]
with the following properties:

1. the induced intrinsic metric is the flat metric of the rectangle;
2. the image is embedded with positive clearance;
3. the surface is made from finitely many planar pieces and cylindrical pieces meeting \(C^1\)-smoothly along rulings;
4. it has a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings, meaning each ruling joins one boundary component to the other and maps to a straight segment in \(\mathbb R^3\).

The construction is Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction. In its standard notation, for total length \(l\), half-width \(b\), and cylinder diameter parameter \(d>0\), the construction works whenever
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
It consists of three planar parts and three semicylindrical parts; the cylindrical generators and the planar ruling lines form a continuous full-width ruling foliation. Because planes and cylinders are developable and are glued tangent-to-tangent along rulings, the resulting surface is \(C^1\) and path-isometric to the original rectangle. The strict inequality above gives positive separation between the distinct pieces, hence positive clearance.

For our normalization, take \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\). Since \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Thus the construction gives a width-one, length-\(\mu\), embedded path-isometric Möbius band with the stated ruling foliation.

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives the same statement for width \(c\) and length
\[
L=\mu c.
\]
Let
\[
M_{L,c}=([0,L]\times[0,c])/(0,Y)\sim(L,c-Y).
\]
The lift to the infinite strip \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) will be denoted
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
It is invariant under
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]
and it is quotient-injective:
\[
E(P)=E(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

The lifted rulings can be written uniquely as
\[
R_x=[B_x,T_x],
\qquad
B_x=(x,0),\qquad
T_x=(h(x),c),
\]
where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a continuous strictly increasing homeomorphism. The deck map sends \(R_x\) to \(R_{\phi(x)}\), where
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,
\]
and the top endpoint relation gives
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
The quantity \(|h(x)-x|\) is bounded globally; indeed, on a fundamental leaf interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), set
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
The relation \(h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x)\) extends this bound to all \(x\).

---

## 8. Invariant clean secant-PL approximation

We now prove the PL approximation lemma needed for the construction.

**Lemma.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be a lifted Sadowsky band of width \(c\), length \(L\), and deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Let \(S\) be the global bound for the horizontal spans of the rulings:
\[
|h(x)-x|\le S.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z;
   \]
2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,
\qquad
2\rho<\varepsilon,
\]
and small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Pick \(x_0\). Since \(\phi(x_0)>x_0\), choose a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so fine that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\qquad
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for every \(i\). Include among the \(x_i\)’s all seam rulings of the finitely many planar and cylindrical pieces in one quotient period.

Extend the sequence by the deck rule
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]
Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),
\qquad
T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The rulings
\[
R_i=[B_i,T_i]
\]
cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]

Now triangulate anti-periodically under the half-twist. For \(i=0,\dots,N-1\), triangulate \(Q_i\) by the diagonal
\[
B_iT_{i+1}.
\]
Since \(H\) swaps the two boundary components, it sends this diagonal to the opposite diagonal in \(Q_{i+N}\):
\[
H(B_iT_{i+1})=T_{i+N}B_{i+N+1}.
\]
Therefore triangulate \(Q_{i+N}\) by the diagonal
\[
T_{i+N}B_{i+N+1}.
\]
Extend by \(H^2\). This gives a genuinely \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),
\qquad
F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. The vertex assignment and the anti-periodic diagonal choice imply
\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

We next prove embeddedness on the quotient. On the compact quotient Möbius band, the smooth Sadowsky surface is \(C^1\) embedded. In ruling coordinates, each smooth piece has the form
\[
G(u,s)=(1-s)\gamma_0(u)+s\gamma_1(u),
\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
where the curves \(\gamma_0,\gamma_1\) are \(C^1\) on each planar or cylindrical piece and match \(C^1\) across seam rulings. Because the seam rulings were included in the partition, every secant strip lies inside one smooth piece. As the maximum of the bottom and top endpoint increments tends to \(0\), the two affine triangles spanning a strip converge uniformly and in tangent plane to the corresponding ruled surface strip.

A compact \(C^1\) embedded surface has local graph neighborhoods and a tubular neighborhood in the following elementary sense: finitely many coordinate neighborhoods cover the quotient, in each of which the surface is a \(C^1\) graph over a plane; outside a sufficiently small neighborhood of the diagonal in the domain, distinct image points are separated by a positive distance. For sufficiently small \(\rho\), every secant triangle lies in one of these graph neighborhoods and is itself a graph over the same plane with small slope, while the whole PL surface is uniformly close to the smooth embedded surface. Therefore no local folding and no nonlocal self-intersection can occur. Thus the induced quotient PL map is embedded.

Equivalently, the nearest local graph projection from the PL surface to the smooth surface gives a map of the quotient Möbius band uniformly close to the identity and locally injective; by compactness and the positive separation of non-near points, it is globally injective. Lifting back to the infinite strip gives quotient-injectivity:
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

Now we prove the edge estimates.

Boundary edges are chords of the smooth boundary curves. Since \(E\) is path-isometric, each such chord has length at most the corresponding flat boundary arc. Hence boundary edges are nonexpanded.

Ruling edges are exact: if the edge is \(B_iT_i\), then \(E(B_i)E(T_i)\) is a straight image of a flat ruling, so
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Consider a forward diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Put
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\qquad
r=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]
Since \(T_i\) and \(T_{i+1}\) differ horizontally by \(<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}<\rho.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r-\rho\\
&\ge d-2\rho\\
&>d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The opposite diagonal \(T_iB_{i+1}\) is estimated identically, using the bottom increment \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\).

Finally, every ruling has horizontal span at most \(S\), and every diagonal has horizontal span \(<S+\rho<D\). This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

## 9. Anisotropic pullback

Let \(\beta>\sqrt3\) be given. Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Take the width-one Sadowsky band of length \(\mu\), and let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound. Choose a number
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Choose \(a<1\), close enough to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]
Define
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}.
\]
Then \(c>1\), and if
\[
L=\mu c,
\]
then
\[
L=a\beta.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\). It now has width \(c\), length \(L=\mu c\), and ruling-span bound \(cS_\mu\). Apply the secant-PL lemma with transverse horizontal-span bound
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

We must choose \(a\) and the secant error \(\varepsilon\) so that transverse edges become strictly lengthened after pullback. For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)
=
\sqrt{c^2s^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{\frac{c^2s^2}{a^2}+1},
\qquad
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}.
\]
At \(a=1\), one has \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and
\[
\sqrt{c^2s^2+c^2}
>
\sqrt{c^2s^2+1}
\]
for every \(s\). Hence \(\Delta_1(s)>0\) on the compact interval \([0,D_\mu]\). By continuity, for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\), still
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]
Apply the secant-PL lemma with this \(\varepsilon\).

Now define the anisotropic affine map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Then
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
(ax+L,c-cy)
=
H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Let
\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

We verify the squeeze inequalities.

### Boundary edges

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Its image \(A(p)A(q)\) has flat length
\[
a|p-q|.
\]
Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Nonboundary edges

Let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of the edge \(A(p)A(q)\). By construction,
\[
0\le t\le cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), with \(0\le s\le D_\mu\). In the scaled Sadowsky strip,
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}.
\]
In the original strip \(\Sigma\),
\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}.
\]
The secant estimate gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon.
\]
Because \(\varepsilon<m\le\Delta_a(s)\),
\[
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}
=
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

### Deck-orbit injectivity

Finally, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\circ G_\beta=H_{L,c}\circ A\) and \(A\) is injective.

Hence \(f\) realizes \(\beta\).

Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\) was arbitrary, every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

Combining Part I and Part II, the realized numbers are exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is
\[
\boxed{\sqrt3}.
\]

## The upper bound

We prove: **for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), \(\beta\) is realized** in the exact clean PL sense of the problem.

### 1. Sadowsky input

We use the following classical Sadowsky three-cylinder construction.

**Sadowsky theorem, in the needed form.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there is an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), with positive clearance and a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. The planar/cylindrical pieces are bounded by ruling leaves, and the map is affine-isometric on planar pieces and cylindrical-isometric on cylindrical pieces.

This follows from Sadowsky’s construction with total length \(l=\mu\), half-width \(b=1/2\), and rod diameter parameter \(d>0\) satisfying

\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]

which is possible exactly because \(\mu>\sqrt3\). The construction consists of planar and semicylindrical developable pieces joined \(C^1\) along full-width generator rulings. The strict inequality gives positive clearance.

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives the same object with width \(c\) and length

\[
L=\mu c.
\]

Let

\[
M_{L,c}=([0,L]\times[0,c])/(0,Y)\sim(L,c-Y),
\]

and lift the Sadowsky embedding to

\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3.
\]

The deck transformation is

\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]

and

\[
E\circ H=E.
\]

Because the quotient band is embedded,

\[
E(P)=E(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

---

## 2. Ruling coordinates on the lift

The full-width ruling foliation lifts to straight segments

\[
R_x=[B_x,T_x],
\qquad
B_x=(x,0),
\qquad
T_x=(h(x),c),
\]

where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous and strictly increasing. Strict monotonicity follows because two lifted ruling segments cannot cross in the flat strip.

The deck map sends \(R_x\) to another ruling. Since

\[
H(B_x)=(x+L,c),
\qquad
H(T_x)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]

we have

\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},
\qquad
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]

Thus the top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) is \(x+L\), so

\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]

Consequently,

\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]

Also \(\phi(x)>x\); otherwise monotonicity of \(\phi\) would contradict \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\).

Fix \(x_0\). Since

\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]

the horizontal span \(|h(x)-x|\) is \(\phi\)-periodic. Hence

\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|
\]

is a global bound for the horizontal spans of all rulings.

---

# 3. Invariant secant-PL approximation lemma

We prove the key lemma.

### Lemma

Let \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) be the lifted Sadowsky band above. For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map

\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]

such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
   \]
2. Every boundary edge is nonexpanded.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|
   \ge
   |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

---

## 3.1. Choosing an invariant partition by rulings

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that

\[
S+\rho<D,
\qquad
2\rho<\varepsilon,
\]

and small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Choose a finite partition

\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]

such that:

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\qquad
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

and such that every Sadowsky seam ruling in the quotient is included among the \(x_i\).

Extend the sequence by

\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]

Then

\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Thus the bi-infinite sequence \((x_i)_{i\in\mathbb Z}\) is locally finite and tends to \(\pm\infty\).

Set

\[
B_i=(x_i,0),
\qquad
T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]

The consecutive rulings

\[
R_i=[B_i,T_i]
\]

cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals

\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i).
\]

Because \(h\) is increasing, these quadrilaterals have disjoint interiors and cover \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\).

---

## 3.2. Anti-periodic diagonal choice

There are two possible diagonals in \(Q_i\):

\[
d_i^+=B_iT_{i+1},
\qquad
d_i^-=B_{i+1}T_i.
\]

The half-twist \(H\) swaps the two diagonal types. Indeed,

\[
H(d_i^+)=d_{i+N}^-,
\qquad
H(d_i^-)=d_{i+N}^+.
\]

Therefore a uniform diagonal choice is not \(H\)-invariant. We choose diagonals anti-periodically:

\[
d_i=d_i^+
\quad\text{for }0\le i<N,
\]

\[
d_{i+N}=H(d_i)=d_{i+N}^-,
\]

and then extend by \(H^2\). Equivalently,

\[
d_{i+2N}=d_i.
\]

This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

---

## 3.3. Defining the secant map

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by

\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),
\qquad
F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]

and extend affinely over every triangle of \(\mathcal T_0\).

Because \(E\circ H=E\), and because the diagonal choices are \(H\)-invariant, the affine extensions agree under \(H\). Hence

\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

---

## 3.4. Embeddedness and quotient-injectivity

We claim that, after taking \(\rho\) sufficiently small, the quotient map

\[
\bar F_0:M_{L,c}\to\mathbb R^3
\]

is embedded.

Here is a direct proof using the planar/cylindrical nature of Sadowsky’s surface.

Because all seam rulings are included in the partition, each small quadrilateral \(Q_i\) lies inside one Sadowsky piece, except along its boundary.

### Planar pieces

On a planar piece, the Sadowsky map is the restriction of a Euclidean isometry from the flat development to a plane. Therefore the affine secant map \(F_0\) agrees exactly with \(E\) on every triangle contained in that planar piece. Hence no self-intersections are introduced there.

### Cylindrical pieces

On a cylindrical piece, after a rigid motion, the image lies on a circular cylinder. The rulings are generator segments. Consecutive chosen rulings determine a chordal side face of the inscribed polygonal cylinder.

For \(\rho\) small, the angular separation of consecutive generators is \(<\pi\). The side faces of an inscribed polygonal cylinder over a strictly convex circular arc meet only along common generator lines. Each pair of triangles in a quadrilateral \(Q_i\) lies inside one such side face. Therefore the secant approximation is embedded inside each cylindrical piece. Radial projection back to the cylinder is a homeomorphism onto the corresponding cylindrical strip.

### Across seams

At a seam ruling, the two adjacent Sadowsky pieces meet \(C^1\). Hence in a small neighborhood of the seam, the original surface is a \(C^1\) graph over the common tangent plane, with the seam projecting to a straight line dividing the two sides. For \(\rho\) sufficiently small, the neighboring secant facets have planes arbitrarily close to this common tangent plane and project into the two corresponding half-neighborhoods. Thus they meet only along the seam ruling.

### Nonlocal intersections

Away from seam neighborhoods and from adjacent pieces, the compact embedded Sadowsky surface has positive separation. Since the planar secants are exact and the cylindrical secants converge uniformly to the cylindrical pieces as \(\rho\to0\), sufficiently fine secants stay inside this clearance. Hence no new nonlocal intersections occur.

Thus \(\bar F_0\) is embedded. Since \(F_0\) is its \(H\)-periodic lift,

\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\iff
P=H^k(Q)
\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

This proves quotient-injectivity.

---

## 3.5. Boundary edges are nonexpanded

Consider a bottom boundary edge \(B_iB_{i+1}\). Its image is the chord between \(E(B_i)\) and \(E(B_{i+1})\). Since \(E\) is path-isometric, the boundary arc from \(B_i\) to \(B_{i+1}\) has length

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i.
\]

Therefore

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(B_{i+1})|
\le
x_{i+1}-x_i.
\]

Similarly,

\[
|F_0(T_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
\le
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i).
\]

Thus every boundary edge is nonexpanded.

---

## 3.6. Transverse edge estimates

Ruling edges are exact. Indeed,

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|
=
|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|
=
|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\]

because \(E\) maps each ruling isometrically to a straight segment.

Now consider a plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let

\[
r=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\qquad
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Since

\[
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

we have

\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]

Also,

\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|
\le
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<\rho.
\]

Therefore

\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=
|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge
|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge
r-\rho \\
&\ge
d-2\rho \\
&>
d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]

The estimate for a minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\) is identical, using the bottom increment

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho.
\]

Hence every transverse edge loses at most \(\varepsilon\).

---

## 3.7. Horizontal span bound

Every ruling edge has horizontal span at most \(S\).

A plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le
|h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<
S+\rho
<
D.
\]

A minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\) satisfies the same estimate using \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\). Therefore every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

This proves the secant-PL lemma.

---

# 4. Pullback to the original strip

Now fix

\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Choose

\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]

Start with the width-one, length-\(\mu\) Sadowsky band. Let \(S_\mu\) be the horizontal ruling-span bound for that normalized band, and choose

\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

We will scale the Sadowsky band to width \(c\) and length \(L=\mu c\), then pull it back anisotropically to \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\).

Choose \(a<1\), close to \(1\), with

\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]

Define

\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}>1,
\qquad
L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]

The scaled band has width \(c\), length \(L\), and ruling-span bound

\[
S=cS_\mu.
\]

Set

\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

Then \(D>S\).

---

## 4.1. The length margin

Let

\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]

Since \(c=a\lambda\), for \(s\in[0,D_\mu]\) define

\[
\Delta_a(s)
=
c\sqrt{s^2+1}
-
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

At \(a=1\), we have \(c=\lambda\), and

\[
\Delta_1(s)
=
\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}
-
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

This is strictly positive because

\[
\lambda^2(s^2+1)>\lambda^2s^2+1
\]

whenever \(\lambda>1\). Since \([0,D_\mu]\) is compact, \(\Delta_1\) has positive minimum. Therefore, for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),

\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]

Choose

\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]

Apply the secant-PL lemma to the scaled width-\(c\), length-\(L\) Sadowsky band, with parameters \(D=cD_\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\). We obtain \(\mathcal T_0\) and \(F_0\).

---

## 4.2. The anisotropic conjugacy

Define

\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]

Then

\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
A(x+\beta,1-y)
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy).
\]

Since \(a\beta=L\),

\[
A\circ G_\beta
=
H\circ A.
\]

Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H\).

Define

\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ A.
\]

Because \(A\) is affine and sends boundary to boundary, \(\mathcal T\) is a clean, locally finite, \(G_\beta\)-invariant triangulation of \(\Sigma\). Also \(f\) is affine on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

---

# 5. Verification of the squeeze inequalities

## 5.1. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\), and

\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
a|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\le
a|p-q|.
\]

Because \(a<1\),

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|<|p-q|.
\]

So the boundary edge is strictly shortened.

---

## 5.2. Transverse edges are strictly lengthened

Let \(pq\) be a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T_0\). Let \(t\) be its horizontal span in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). The secant construction gives

\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]

Write

\[
t=cs,
\qquad
0\le s<D_\mu.
\]

Since the vertical separation after applying \(A\) is \(c\),

\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
=
c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]

The original horizontal span is \(t/a\), and the original vertical separation is \(1\), so

\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

By the secant edge estimate,

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
\]

Therefore

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]

Since \(\varepsilon<m\le \Delta_a(s)\),

\[
c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Hence \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every clean triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

---

# 6. Quotient-injectivity of the final map

For \(p,q\in\Sigma\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq) \\
&\iff
Ap=H^k(Aq)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]

because \(A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A\).

Thus \(f\) satisfies the required orbit-identification condition.

Therefore \(\beta\) is realized.

Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\) was arbitrary, every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## Skeptical note

The only external theorem used is the stated Sadowsky three-cylinder existence theorem. The potentially delicate PL point is quotient-injectivity of the secant approximation. That is handled above by using the actual planar/cylindrical structure: planar pieces are exact, cylindrical pieces become embedded inscribed polygonal-cylinder strips, \(C^1\) seam neighborhoods are local graphs, and positive clearance prevents nonlocal intersections. Thus no additional smooth Möbius-band black box is being used.

  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Theorem

If \(\beta>0\) is realized, then  
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]

Throughout, “decreases” and “increases” mean strictly.

---

## 1. Cevian-lengthening and face-to-face refinement

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) and opposite vertex \(a\). Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
The squeeze assumptions give
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0,
\]
because the two nonboundary edges are lengthened. Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, hence lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0 .
\]
Therefore every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened:
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)| .
\]

Now refine the triangulation to make it face-to-face. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: if two such edges crossed in their relative interiors, the two adjacent triangle interiors would occupy two open half-planes with nonparallel boundary lines, hence would overlap in a small sector. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then since each joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), they have the same endpoints and are the same edge. Thus the only possible non-face-to-face behavior is along boundary edges.

For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness implies only finitely many such subdivision points occur on each boundary edge. The preceding cevian lemma shows every new nonboundary edge is lengthened, while every new boundary subedge is shortened because \(f\) is affine on the original boundary edge. Hence the refined triangulation is still clean, \(G_\beta\)-invariant, and squeeze-compatible. We use this face-to-face refinement from now on.

---

## 2. Quotient and PL embedding

Let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is a Möbius band whose boundary has flat length \(2\beta\). The realization condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous injective map
\[
F:M\to\mathbb R^3 .
\]
Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\ne0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle, then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

Consequently, no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity on \(\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, so the quotient cellulation of \(M\) has finitely many triangles. Thus \(F\) is a finite PL embedding, affine on every quotient triangle. Also, since every boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(F\) is affine on boundary edges, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in path length.

---

## 3. Leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider the family of cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. Abstractly, this family is an interval. The endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary side edges. Glue two such interval endpoints whenever the corresponding side edges are the same edge of the triangulation. This gives a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), the quotient leaf graph.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of the triangles whose cevian-intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the adjacent triangle belongs to the same component by construction. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan
\[
T_1,\dots,T_m,
\]
and consecutive triangles share nonboundary edges except at the two extreme boundary edges. Thus all triangles incident to a given boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Therefore each \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M\). Hence
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1 .
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering. No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf: if a leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even deck power translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd deck power would force simultaneously
\[
x_1-x_0=(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0-x_1=(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Thus the covering has infinite fibers, so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R .
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image is a nondegenerate line segment, by the cevian-lengthening lemma. Hence it has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\), varying continuously with the leaf. If a leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image orientation reverses. Therefore, after one circuit of the quotient leaf circle,
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}} .
\]

---

## 4. Oriented-line Borsuk–Ulam lemma

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\), and extend them by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]
satisfies
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

If \((a,b)\) never vanished, then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
would define a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2),
\]
so that middle-circle map has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to a constant map, contradiction. Hence some \((s,\delta)\) has
\[
a(s,\delta)=b(s,\delta)=0.
\]
The zero cannot occur at \(\delta=0\) or \(\delta=1\), so \(0<\delta<1\), giving two distinct quotient leaves.

Now \(a=0\) says the two line directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
For nonparallel lines, the vanishing of this scalar triple product is exactly coplanarity. Since the directions are perpendicular, the lines are nonparallel; coplanar nonparallel lines intersect. Thus the support-line family contains two distinct perpendicular intersecting support lines.

---

## 5. Metric endgame

Choose two quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves so that the image segment of \(B\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) is contained in a closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put \(Q=F(q)\). Since the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the cevian-lengthening lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
where \(|B|_0\) is the flat length of a lifted leaf. Hence
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(T\), forcing \(y=0\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
t=\frac{\ell_0(D)-\ell_0(H)}2 .
\]

We do not assume that \(t\) is the horizontal displacement of a preferred lift. Instead, lift \(T\) to a segment joining \((x,0)\) to \((x+s,1)\). On the quotient boundary, the top endpoint corresponds to bottom coordinate \(x+s-\beta\) modulo \(2\beta\). Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), one has
\[
s\equiv t \pmod{2\beta}
\quad\text{or}\quad
s\equiv -t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Thus \(|s|\ge |t|\), and therefore the actual flat leaf length satisfies
\[
|T|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
By cevian-lengthening,
\[
L>|T|_0\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Because \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary reflected-triangle estimate gives
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
and therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply
\[
t<\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]
while the second would imply
\[
t>\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]
a contradiction. Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

So every realized \(\beta\) is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\). No degeneracy remains: the triangulation was made face-to-face, quotient finiteness and PL embeddedness were proved, shared boundary endpoints are harmless, and the metric argument uses boundary arc lengths rather than a minimal-displacement assumption.

## The upper bound

We prove: **for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), \(\beta\) is realized** in the exact clean PL sense of the problem.

### 1. Sadowsky input

We use the following classical Sadowsky three-cylinder construction.

**Sadowsky theorem, in the needed form.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there is an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), with positive clearance and a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. The planar/cylindrical pieces are bounded by ruling leaves, and the map is affine-isometric on planar pieces and cylindrical-isometric on cylindrical pieces.

This follows from Sadowsky’s construction with total length \(l=\mu\), half-width \(b=1/2\), and rod diameter parameter \(d>0\) satisfying

\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]

which is possible exactly because \(\mu>\sqrt3\). The construction consists of planar and semicylindrical developable pieces joined \(C^1\) along full-width generator rulings. The strict inequality gives positive clearance.

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives the same object with width \(c\) and length

\[
L=\mu c.
\]

Let

\[
M_{L,c}=([0,L]\times[0,c])/(0,Y)\sim(L,c-Y),
\]

and lift the Sadowsky embedding to

\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3.
\]

The deck transformation is

\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]

and

\[
E\circ H=E.
\]

Because the quotient band is embedded,

\[
E(P)=E(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

---

## 2. Ruling coordinates on the lift

The full-width ruling foliation lifts to straight segments

\[
R_x=[B_x,T_x],
\qquad
B_x=(x,0),
\qquad
T_x=(h(x),c),
\]

where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous and strictly increasing. Strict monotonicity follows because two lifted ruling segments cannot cross in the flat strip.

The deck map sends \(R_x\) to another ruling. Since

\[
H(B_x)=(x+L,c),
\qquad
H(T_x)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]

we have

\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},
\qquad
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]

Thus the top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) is \(x+L\), so

\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]

Consequently,

\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]

Also \(\phi(x)>x\); otherwise monotonicity of \(\phi\) would contradict \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\).

Fix \(x_0\). Since

\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]

the horizontal span \(|h(x)-x|\) is \(\phi\)-periodic. Hence

\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|
\]

is a global bound for the horizontal spans of all rulings.

---

# 3. Invariant secant-PL approximation lemma

We prove the key lemma.

### Lemma

Let \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) be the lifted Sadowsky band above. For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map

\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]

such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
   \]
2. Every boundary edge is nonexpanded.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|
   \ge
   |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

---

## 3.1. Choosing an invariant partition by rulings

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that

\[
S+\rho<D,
\qquad
2\rho<\varepsilon,
\]

and small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Choose a finite partition

\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]

such that:

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\qquad
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

and such that every Sadowsky seam ruling in the quotient is included among the \(x_i\).

Extend the sequence by

\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]

Then

\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Thus the bi-infinite sequence \((x_i)_{i\in\mathbb Z}\) is locally finite and tends to \(\pm\infty\).

Set

\[
B_i=(x_i,0),
\qquad
T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]

The consecutive rulings

\[
R_i=[B_i,T_i]
\]

cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals

\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i).
\]

Because \(h\) is increasing, these quadrilaterals have disjoint interiors and cover \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\).

---

## 3.2. Anti-periodic diagonal choice

There are two possible diagonals in \(Q_i\):

\[
d_i^+=B_iT_{i+1},
\qquad
d_i^-=B_{i+1}T_i.
\]

The half-twist \(H\) swaps the two diagonal types. Indeed,

\[
H(d_i^+)=d_{i+N}^-,
\qquad
H(d_i^-)=d_{i+N}^+.
\]

Therefore a uniform diagonal choice is not \(H\)-invariant. We choose diagonals anti-periodically:

\[
d_i=d_i^+
\quad\text{for }0\le i<N,
\]

\[
d_{i+N}=H(d_i)=d_{i+N}^-,
\]

and then extend by \(H^2\). Equivalently,

\[
d_{i+2N}=d_i.
\]

This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

---

## 3.3. Defining the secant map

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by

\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),
\qquad
F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]

and extend affinely over every triangle of \(\mathcal T_0\).

Because \(E\circ H=E\), and because the diagonal choices are \(H\)-invariant, the affine extensions agree under \(H\). Hence

\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

---

## 3.4. Embeddedness and quotient-injectivity

We claim that, after taking \(\rho\) sufficiently small, the quotient map

\[
\bar F_0:M_{L,c}\to\mathbb R^3
\]

is embedded.

Here is a direct proof using the planar/cylindrical nature of Sadowsky’s surface.

Because all seam rulings are included in the partition, each small quadrilateral \(Q_i\) lies inside one Sadowsky piece, except along its boundary.

### Planar pieces

On a planar piece, the Sadowsky map is the restriction of a Euclidean isometry from the flat development to a plane. Therefore the affine secant map \(F_0\) agrees exactly with \(E\) on every triangle contained in that planar piece. Hence no self-intersections are introduced there.

### Cylindrical pieces

On a cylindrical piece, after a rigid motion, the image lies on a circular cylinder. The rulings are generator segments. Consecutive chosen rulings determine a chordal side face of the inscribed polygonal cylinder.

For \(\rho\) small, the angular separation of consecutive generators is \(<\pi\). The side faces of an inscribed polygonal cylinder over a strictly convex circular arc meet only along common generator lines. Each pair of triangles in a quadrilateral \(Q_i\) lies inside one such side face. Therefore the secant approximation is embedded inside each cylindrical piece. Radial projection back to the cylinder is a homeomorphism onto the corresponding cylindrical strip.

### Across seams

At a seam ruling, the two adjacent Sadowsky pieces meet \(C^1\). Hence in a small neighborhood of the seam, the original surface is a \(C^1\) graph over the common tangent plane, with the seam projecting to a straight line dividing the two sides. For \(\rho\) sufficiently small, the neighboring secant facets have planes arbitrarily close to this common tangent plane and project into the two corresponding half-neighborhoods. Thus they meet only along the seam ruling.

### Nonlocal intersections

Away from seam neighborhoods and from adjacent pieces, the compact embedded Sadowsky surface has positive separation. Since the planar secants are exact and the cylindrical secants converge uniformly to the cylindrical pieces as \(\rho\to0\), sufficiently fine secants stay inside this clearance. Hence no new nonlocal intersections occur.

Thus \(\bar F_0\) is embedded. Since \(F_0\) is its \(H\)-periodic lift,

\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\iff
P=H^k(Q)
\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

This proves quotient-injectivity.

---

## 3.5. Boundary edges are nonexpanded

Consider a bottom boundary edge \(B_iB_{i+1}\). Its image is the chord between \(E(B_i)\) and \(E(B_{i+1})\). Since \(E\) is path-isometric, the boundary arc from \(B_i\) to \(B_{i+1}\) has length

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i.
\]

Therefore

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(B_{i+1})|
\le
x_{i+1}-x_i.
\]

Similarly,

\[
|F_0(T_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
\le
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i).
\]

Thus every boundary edge is nonexpanded.

---

## 3.6. Transverse edge estimates

Ruling edges are exact. Indeed,

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|
=
|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|
=
|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\]

because \(E\) maps each ruling isometrically to a straight segment.

Now consider a plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let

\[
r=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\qquad
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Since

\[
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

we have

\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]

Also,

\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|
\le
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<\rho.
\]

Therefore

\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=
|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge
|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge
r-\rho \\
&\ge
d-2\rho \\
&>
d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]

The estimate for a minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\) is identical, using the bottom increment

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho.
\]

Hence every transverse edge loses at most \(\varepsilon\).

---

## 3.7. Horizontal span bound

Every ruling edge has horizontal span at most \(S\).

A plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le
|h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<
S+\rho
<
D.
\]

A minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\) satisfies the same estimate using \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\). Therefore every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

This proves the secant-PL lemma.

---

# 4. Pullback to the original strip

Now fix

\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Choose

\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]

Start with the width-one, length-\(\mu\) Sadowsky band. Let \(S_\mu\) be the horizontal ruling-span bound for that normalized band, and choose

\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

We will scale the Sadowsky band to width \(c\) and length \(L=\mu c\), then pull it back anisotropically to \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\).

Choose \(a<1\), close to \(1\), with

\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]

Define

\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}>1,
\qquad
L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]

The scaled band has width \(c\), length \(L\), and ruling-span bound

\[
S=cS_\mu.
\]

Set

\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

Then \(D>S\).

---

## 4.1. The length margin

Let

\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]

Since \(c=a\lambda\), for \(s\in[0,D_\mu]\) define

\[
\Delta_a(s)
=
c\sqrt{s^2+1}
-
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

At \(a=1\), we have \(c=\lambda\), and

\[
\Delta_1(s)
=
\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}
-
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

This is strictly positive because

\[
\lambda^2(s^2+1)>\lambda^2s^2+1
\]

whenever \(\lambda>1\). Since \([0,D_\mu]\) is compact, \(\Delta_1\) has positive minimum. Therefore, for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),

\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]

Choose

\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]

Apply the secant-PL lemma to the scaled width-\(c\), length-\(L\) Sadowsky band, with parameters \(D=cD_\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\). We obtain \(\mathcal T_0\) and \(F_0\).

---

## 4.2. The anisotropic conjugacy

Define

\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]

Then

\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
A(x+\beta,1-y)
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy).
\]

Since \(a\beta=L\),

\[
A\circ G_\beta
=
H\circ A.
\]

Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H\).

Define

\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ A.
\]

Because \(A\) is affine and sends boundary to boundary, \(\mathcal T\) is a clean, locally finite, \(G_\beta\)-invariant triangulation of \(\Sigma\). Also \(f\) is affine on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

---

# 5. Verification of the squeeze inequalities

## 5.1. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\), and

\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
a|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\le
a|p-q|.
\]

Because \(a<1\),

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|<|p-q|.
\]

So the boundary edge is strictly shortened.

---

## 5.2. Transverse edges are strictly lengthened

Let \(pq\) be a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T_0\). Let \(t\) be its horizontal span in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). The secant construction gives

\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]

Write

\[
t=cs,
\qquad
0\le s<D_\mu.
\]

Since the vertical separation after applying \(A\) is \(c\),

\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
=
c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]

The original horizontal span is \(t/a\), and the original vertical separation is \(1\), so

\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

By the secant edge estimate,

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
\]

Therefore

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]

Since \(\varepsilon<m\le \Delta_a(s)\),

\[
c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Hence \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every clean triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

---

# 6. Quotient-injectivity of the final map

For \(p,q\in\Sigma\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq) \\
&\iff
Ap=H^k(Aq)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]

because \(A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A\).

Thus \(f\) satisfies the required orbit-identification condition.

Therefore \(\beta\) is realized.

Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\) was arbitrary, every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## Skeptical note

The only external theorem used is the stated Sadowsky three-cylinder existence theorem. The potentially delicate PL point is quotient-injectivity of the secant approximation. That is handled above by using the actual planar/cylindrical structure: planar pieces are exact, cylindrical pieces become embedded inscribed polygonal-cylinder strips, \(C^1\) seam neighborhoods are local graphs, and positive clearance prevents nonlocal intersections. Thus no additional smooth Möbius-band black box is being used.

## Short answer

Route A works unconditionally. The prior “smooth ruled band” black box can be replaced by Sadowsky’s explicit three-cylinder construction, in the slightly weaker \(C^1\), piecewise-\(C^\infty\) developable category; that is already enough for the clean-PL secant-and-pullback argument. If one insists on a \(C^\infty\) source theorem, this is the classical Sadowsky/Halpern–Weaver smoothing theorem, but the PL proof below avoids any hidden smooth/PL mismatch.

---

## Route A: Sadowsky input and clean-PL realization

### Classical input used

Use the following form of Sadowsky’s construction.

**Sadowsky three-cylinder theorem, sufficient form.**  
Let \(l>0\), \(b>0\), and choose \(d>0\) so that

\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Then the flat rectangle of length \(l\) and width \(2b\), with opposite short sides identified with a half-twist, has an embedded isometric developable Möbius realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). The realization is \(C^1\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), made from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces, and has a continuous full-width ruling foliation by straight generators.

This is exactly Sadowsky’s 1930 three-rod construction: in the Hinz–Fried translation, the rods have diameters \(d,d,2d\), the surface consists of planar and semicylindrical sections, the midline length is \(l=3L\), and the maximal half-width satisfies

\[
b\le \frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

The strict inequality gives positive clearance, so there is no boundary contact/overlap. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

Now fix \(\mu>\sqrt3\). Set \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\). Choose

\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]

Then

\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Thus Sadowsky gives an embedded isometric Möbius band of width \(1\), length \(\mu\), with a continuous full-width ruling foliation. Its lift

\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]

is invariant under

\[
H_{\mu,1}(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y)
\]

and quotient-injective. The full-width rulings lift to segments

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),1)]
\]

with \(h\) continuous and strictly increasing. On one fundamental interval, \(|h(x)-x|\) is bounded.

For comparison, Halpern–Weaver is the classical smooth source for the smooth paper-band version, and Schwartz’s modern account uses the standard definition of a smooth embedded paper Möbius band and recalls the continuous bend foliation for smooth embedded paper bands. ([ams.org](https://www.ams.org/journals/tran/1977-230-00/S0002-9947-1977-0474388-1/home.html?utm_source=openai)) But the proof below only needs the \(C^1\), piecewise-smooth Sadowsky band, so there is no category gap.

---

## PL secant lemma, in the needed \(C^1\) form

Let

\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]

on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). Suppose \(E\) is a quotient-injective embedded isometric developable band of width \(c\), length \(L\), invariant under \(H_{L,c}\), with continuous full-width rulings

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]

Let

\[
S=\sup |h(x)-x|
\]

over one fundamental interval. Then for every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), affine on each triangle, such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective.
2. Boundary edges are nonexpanded.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

The proof is the same secant proof as in the prior t2 draft: choose adjacent rulings \(R_i=[B_i,T_i]\) so close that both bottom and top endpoint increments are \(<\rho\), triangulate each clean quadrilateral between \(R_i\) and \(R_{i+1}\), and put \(F_0\) on vertices equal to \(E\). Ruling edges are exact; diagonal edges lose at most \(2\rho\) by the triangle inequality; boundary chords are no longer than their boundary arcs. Embeddedness follows from the standard inscribed-PL approximation theorem for compact \(C^1\) embedded surfaces: for a sufficiently fine partition, the secant surface lies in a tubular neighborhood and projects homeomorphically to the original band. The Sadowsky seams cause no problem because the surface is \(C^1\), and one may include seam rulings among the partition rulings.

---

## Pullback to the required clean category

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose

\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]

Use the Sadowsky band of normalized aspect \(\mu\), and let \(D_\mu>S_\mu\) be a ruling-span bound plus margin. Choose \(a<1\), close to \(1\), with

\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta},
\qquad
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}>1,
\qquad
L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\), so the target has width \(c\) and length \(L\).

Define

\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]

Then

\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]

So \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Now choose \(\varepsilon>0\) small enough that

\[
\varepsilon<
\min_{0\le t\le cD_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right).
\]

This minimum is positive because at \(a=1\) we have \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and positivity persists for \(a\) close enough to \(1\).

Apply the PL secant lemma to get \(F_0\), and define

\[
f=F_0\circ A.
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]

Then \(\mathcal T\) is a locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

### Boundary contraction

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(A(p)A(q)\) has flat length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le a|p-q|<|p-q|.
\]

So the boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Transverse expansion

If \(pq\) is a transverse edge, let \(t\le cD_\mu\) be its target horizontal span after applying \(A\). Then

\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2+c^2},
\]

while

\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}.
\]

By the choice of \(\varepsilon\),

\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]

Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

### Quotient-injectivity

Because \(F_0\) is quotient-injective and \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\),

\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\iff Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore \(f\) realizes \(\beta\). Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\) was arbitrary, every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## Route B: direct triangular PL model

Let

\[
u=\frac{\beta}{3},
\]

and consider the clean triangle with vertices

\[
(0,0),\qquad (2u,0),\qquad (u,1).
\]

Its boundary edge has length

\[
\ell=2u=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\]

and its two transverse edges have length

\[
r=\sqrt{1+u^2}=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]

Indeed,

\[
r<\ell
\iff
1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}<\frac{4\beta^2}{9}
\iff
\beta^2>3.
\]

So for \(\beta>\sqrt3\), one can choose \(s\) with

\[
r<s<\ell,
\]

and a single triangle could be affinely mapped to an equilateral triangle of side \(s\), giving the desired edge inequalities.

The obstruction is global injectivity. The natural three-triangle quotient has only three vertex orbits. Write

\[
B_n=(2nu,0),\qquad T_n=((2n+1)u,1).
\]

Then \(G_\beta(B_n)=T_{n+1}\) and \(G_\beta(T_n)=B_{n+2}\), so the quotient has three vertex classes. The three fundamental triangles may be taken as

\[
(B_0,B_1,T_0),\qquad (T_0,T_1,B_1),\qquad (B_1,B_2,T_1).
\]

Any map using only those three vertex images sends every edge with the same pair of endpoint classes to the same straight segment. But in this complex, boundary edges and transverse glued edges share the same endpoint pairs. Hence a boundary edge and an interior transverse edge necessarily overlap in the target. Therefore the raw three-triangle triangular model cannot be quotient-injective, no matter how the three target vertices are perturbed in \(\mathbb R^3\).

A subdivision can in principle separate these coincident edges, but then one must solve a real clearance problem near the three triangular vertices. The Sadowsky/secant construction above is exactly a controlled subdivision-and-perturbation of that triangular limit: the parameter \(d>0\) gives positive clearance, and the strict inequality \(\beta>\sqrt3\) supplies the edge-length margin.

---
