## Theorem

If \(\beta>0\) is realized, then  
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]

Throughout, “decreases” and “increases” mean strictly.

---

## 1. Cevian-lengthening and face-to-face refinement

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) and opposite vertex \(a\). Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
The squeeze assumptions give
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0,
\]
because the two nonboundary edges are lengthened. Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, hence lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0 .
\]
Therefore every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened:
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)| .
\]

Now refine the triangulation to make it face-to-face. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: if two such edges crossed in their relative interiors, the two adjacent triangle interiors would occupy two open half-planes with nonparallel boundary lines, hence would overlap in a small sector. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then since each joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), they have the same endpoints and are the same edge. Thus the only possible non-face-to-face behavior is along boundary edges.

For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness implies only finitely many such subdivision points occur on each boundary edge. The preceding cevian lemma shows every new nonboundary edge is lengthened, while every new boundary subedge is shortened because \(f\) is affine on the original boundary edge. Hence the refined triangulation is still clean, \(G_\beta\)-invariant, and squeeze-compatible. We use this face-to-face refinement from now on.

---

## 2. Quotient and PL embedding

Let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is a Möbius band whose boundary has flat length \(2\beta\). The realization condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous injective map
\[
F:M\to\mathbb R^3 .
\]
Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\ne0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle, then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

Consequently, no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity on \(\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, so the quotient cellulation of \(M\) has finitely many triangles. Thus \(F\) is a finite PL embedding, affine on every quotient triangle. Also, since every boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(F\) is affine on boundary edges, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in path length.

---

## 3. Leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider the family of cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. Abstractly, this family is an interval. The endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary side edges. Glue two such interval endpoints whenever the corresponding side edges are the same edge of the triangulation. This gives a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), the quotient leaf graph.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of the triangles whose cevian-intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the adjacent triangle belongs to the same component by construction. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan
\[
T_1,\dots,T_m,
\]
and consecutive triangles share nonboundary edges except at the two extreme boundary edges. Thus all triangles incident to a given boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Therefore each \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M\). Hence
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1 .
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering. No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf: if a leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even deck power translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd deck power would force simultaneously
\[
x_1-x_0=(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0-x_1=(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Thus the covering has infinite fibers, so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R .
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image is a nondegenerate line segment, by the cevian-lengthening lemma. Hence it has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\), varying continuously with the leaf. If a leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image orientation reverses. Therefore, after one circuit of the quotient leaf circle,
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}} .
\]

---

## 4. Oriented-line Borsuk–Ulam lemma

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\), and extend them by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]
satisfies
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

If \((a,b)\) never vanished, then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
would define a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2),
\]
so that middle-circle map has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to a constant map, contradiction. Hence some \((s,\delta)\) has
\[
a(s,\delta)=b(s,\delta)=0.
\]
The zero cannot occur at \(\delta=0\) or \(\delta=1\), so \(0<\delta<1\), giving two distinct quotient leaves.

Now \(a=0\) says the two line directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
For nonparallel lines, the vanishing of this scalar triple product is exactly coplanarity. Since the directions are perpendicular, the lines are nonparallel; coplanar nonparallel lines intersect. Thus the support-line family contains two distinct perpendicular intersecting support lines.

---

## 5. Metric endgame

Choose two quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves so that the image segment of \(B\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) is contained in a closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put \(Q=F(q)\). Since the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the cevian-lengthening lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
where \(|B|_0\) is the flat length of a lifted leaf. Hence
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(T\), forcing \(y=0\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
t=\frac{\ell_0(D)-\ell_0(H)}2 .
\]

We do not assume that \(t\) is the horizontal displacement of a preferred lift. Instead, lift \(T\) to a segment joining \((x,0)\) to \((x+s,1)\). On the quotient boundary, the top endpoint corresponds to bottom coordinate \(x+s-\beta\) modulo \(2\beta\). Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), one has
\[
s\equiv t \pmod{2\beta}
\quad\text{or}\quad
s\equiv -t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Thus \(|s|\ge |t|\), and therefore the actual flat leaf length satisfies
\[
|T|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
By cevian-lengthening,
\[
L>|T|_0\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Because \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary reflected-triangle estimate gives
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
and therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply
\[
t<\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]
while the second would imply
\[
t>\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]
a contradiction. Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

So every realized \(\beta\) is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\). No degeneracy remains: the triangulation was made face-to-face, quotient finiteness and PL embeddedness were proved, shared boundary endpoints are harmless, and the metric argument uses boundary arc lengths rather than a minimal-displacement assumption.

Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I find the proposed proof **essentially sound**, but two points should be stated more carefully:

1. The quotient of the triangulation is safest to call a **finite regular \(\Delta\)-complex / PL cellulation**; if a literal simplicial complex is desired, take its barycentric subdivision.
2. In the metric endgame, the \(H\)-arc strictness does **not** need a delicate equality-case argument; strict boundary contraction already gives the strict inequality.

Below is a polished repaired proof.

---

## Theorem

If \(\beta>0\) is realized, then  
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]

### 1. Quotient and PL embedding

Let \(\mathcal T\) be the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The realization condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous injective map
\[
F:M\to \mathbb R^3.
\]
Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\ne0\), every point is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were not injective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f|_\Delta\) is injective on every triangle.

Therefore no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\); injectivity on \(\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, so there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of triangles. The quotient is therefore a finite regular PL \(\Delta\)-complex structure on \(M\). No triangle has self-identifications. If one wants an honest simplicial triangulation, barycentrically subdivide this finite \(\Delta\)-complex. The map \(F\) is PL, affine and injective on each original triangle.

---

### 2. Squeeze-cevian lemma

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Thus every cevian from \(a\) to \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened:
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]

Consequences:

- every leaf segment has nonzero image length;
- every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened;
- every boundary edge is strictly shortened;
- since \(F\) is affine on each boundary edge, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in path length.

---

### 3. Leaf graph and oriented support lines

For each triangle, take the interval of its apex-to-boundary cevians. The two endpoints correspond to the two nonboundary side edges. Glue endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary side edges are identified. This gives a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\) in the quotient. Similarly one has an upstairs graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

Each nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so each vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus each component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We show \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of all triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). Across any nonboundary edge, the adjacent triangle belongs to the same component of \(\Lambda_M\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan
\[
T_1,\dots,T_m,
\]
and consecutive triangles share nonboundary edges, except for the two extreme boundary edges. Hence all triangles incident to that boundary vertex lie in the same component. Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a connected covering of a circle. It is not a finite circle cover: no nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) fixes a leaf, because comparing the bottom and top endpoints of a lifted leaf gives an immediate contradiction. Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong \mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This line depends continuously on the leaf parameter: inside a triangle this is affine dependence on the ridge point, and across a shared nonboundary edge the two limiting oriented image segments agree.

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment but with opposite orientation. Therefore, along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\), the oriented support line path satisfies
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

---

### 4. Oriented-line lemma

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines with
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]
Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\), and extend by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]
satisfies
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

If \((a,b)\) never vanished, then normalizing would give a map
\[
\phi:\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1.
\]
On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. But on the middle circle \(\delta=\tfrac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12),
\]
so the middle-circle map has odd degree. This contradicts its homotopy through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map. Hence some pair satisfies
\[
a(s,\delta)=b(s,\delta)=0.
\]

The condition \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the condition \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. Therefore the support-line family contains two perpendicular intersecting support lines.

---

### 5. Leaf displacement and boundary arcs

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top, with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

We claim
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]
If \(t=\beta\), the two endpoints are identified by \(G_\beta\), so their images under \(f\) coincide, contradicting the positive image length of \(J\). The case \(t=-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\).

If \(t>\beta\), then \(J\) intersects \(G_\beta J\) in relative interiors. After translating \(x\) to \(0\), the two segments are
\[
(0,0)\to(t,1)
\]
and
\[
(t+\beta,0)\to(\beta,1),
\]
which meet at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate image segment \(f(J)\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Therefore \(|t|<\beta\).

Now parametrize the quotient boundary by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). The bottom point \((x,0)\) has coordinate \(x\), while the top point \((x+t,1)\) corresponds to bottom coordinate
\[
x+t-\beta.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between the endpoints have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
Replacing the lift \(J\) by \(G_\beta J\) changes \(t\) to \(-t\), so for either chosen boundary arc one may choose the lift so that that arc has length \(\beta+t\).

---

### 6. Metric endgame

By the oriented-line lemma, choose two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Thus at least one of the two image segments lies in a closed ray from \(P\). Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and let
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the cevian lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus, writing
\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T)),
\]
we have
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), forcing \(y=0\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1),
\]
and choose the lift so that the boundary arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Let
\[
L=|F(T)|.
\]
By the cevian lemma,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), boundary contraction gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects the endpoints of \(F(T)\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects the endpoints \(A,C\) of \(F(T)\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(|A-C|=L\) and \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary contraction gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
hence
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let

For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either

or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$.

locally finite collection

$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in

continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$

I found **no fatal flaw** in the 9-periodic construction. The original embeddedness argument was too compressed, but it becomes rigorous with a barycentric-coordinate slice lemma. No modification is needed.

## Theorem — upper bound

For every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), \(\beta\) is realized by a \(9\)-periodic clean PL construction.

### Proof

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), one has \(L<W\). Choose
\[
L<\sigma<W,
\qquad
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L,\;W-\sigma\}>0,
\]
and choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min(\Delta/20,\beta/12).
\]

Define the boundary lengths
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then each \(3\)-block sums to \(W\), and each \(9\)-block sums to \(3W=2\beta\). Let \(c_0=0\), \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\), so \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\). Define
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]

For each \(j\), let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(t\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(t)=(1-t)c_j+t(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(t)-x_j(t)=(1-t)\ell_j+t\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus the intervals \([x_j(t),x_{j+1}(t)]\) tile each horizontal line, and the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\). Splitting \(Q_j\) by the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\) gives clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]

The \(G_\beta\)-action satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5},
\]
so the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) be the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), indices are modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
and \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has exactly the claimed nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

Now choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+z e_3.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\) sufficiently small. Let
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define the nine target vertices
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
For \(\eta,h\) small enough, every target distance between different base clusters is in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\ \sigma+\Delta/4),
\]
and every same-cluster boundary edge has length \(<\lambda/2\).

Define the quotient PL map \(F\) by \(F(v_j)=P_j\), affine on each
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]
The nine target faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

The three non-horizontal quadrilateral panels are
\[
Q_{01}=F_0\cup F_4,\qquad
Q_{12}=F_3\cup F_7,\qquad
Q_{20}=F_1\cup F_6.
\]
They connect respectively the sides \(01\), \(12\), \(20\).

To verify embeddedness rigorously, use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\). For the homothetic triangle of scale \(r\),
\[
T(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\},
\]
one has
\[
T(r)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r)\) opposite \(k\) is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r)=\{\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\}\cap T(r).
\]
Hence adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only in the common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then \(S_{ij}(r)\cap T(s)=\varnothing\).

The horizontal sections are exactly:
\[
Q_{01}\cap\{z\} = S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right),
\quad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
Q_{12}\cap\{z\} = S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{z}{2h}\right),
\quad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
Q_{20}\cap\{z\} = S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{z}{h}\right),
\quad 0\le z\le h.
\]

Now check slices.

- For \(0<z<h\), only \(Q_{12}\) and \(Q_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
  \[
  1+\eta\frac z{2h}>1,\qquad 1-\eta\frac zh<1,
  \]
  so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

- For \(h<z<2h\), only \(Q_{01}\) and \(Q_{12}\) occur. Their scales satisfy
  \[
  \left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
  -
  \left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
  =
  \frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
  \]
  so again the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

- At \(z=0\), the horizontal triangle \([A_0,A_1,A_2]\) meets \(Q_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and \(Q_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\), exactly as intended.

- At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \([M_0,M_1,M_2]\) meets \(Q_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and \(Q_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\). The extra section of \(Q_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), while the middle triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), so that section lies outside the middle triangle and is disjoint from it.

- At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \([C_0,C_1,C_2]\) meets \(Q_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and \(Q_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\), exactly as intended.

Each panel \(Q_{01},Q_{12},Q_{20}\) is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles in each panel meet only in that diagonal. Therefore the nine target triangles intersect only in their prescribed common edges and vertices. In particular, the boundary polygon
\[
C_0,M_0,A_0,A_1,C_1,M_1,M_2,A_2,C_2,C_0
\]
is simple. Thus \(F\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

Now define the lifted map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with \(P_{j+9}=P_j\), and extend affinely over every \(D_j,E_j\). Then
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Hence \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\). Consequently
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

It remains to check the squeeze inequalities. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and its target length is \(<\lambda/2\). If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), while the target edge joins different clusters, so its length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4\). Since \(\sigma\le W-\Delta\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

For a nonboundary quotient edge \(e_j=v_jv_{j+5}\), use the lift \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
Then the flat length is
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct computation gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3},
\]
so every nonboundary flat edge has length \(<L\).

The opposite lift gives the same length: the edge \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), which represents \(e_{j+5}\), has horizontal displacement
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it is covered by the same estimate.

Every target nonboundary edge joins different base clusters, so its length is \(>\sigma-\Delta/4\). Since \(\sigma\ge L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L.
\]
Therefore every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Thus on every clean triangle, the boundary edge is shortened and the two nonboundary edges are lengthened. Hence each affine face map is a squeeze map. Therefore \(\beta\) is realized. \(\square\)

## The lower-bound theorem

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. We prove that  
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]

Let \(\mathcal T_0\) be a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) a realizing map. All length inequalities below are strict.

---

### 1. Cevian lengthening and a harmless PL refinement

First record the basic metric fact used repeatedly.

**Lemma 1.1 — Cevian lengthening.**  
Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle whose boundary edge is \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le 1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
The two nonboundary sides are lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is concave, hence lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Therefore
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]
For a boundary subsegment, affineness gives a constant contraction factor
\[
\frac{|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|}{|r_1-r_0|}<1,
\]
so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) to remove all PL non-face-to-face degeneracies. For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of \(\mathcal T_0\) lying on that boundary edge, and cone those subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so each original triangle is subdivided into finitely many clean triangles.

The refinement is \(G_\beta\)-invariant, locally finite, and countable. By Lemma 1.1, every new nonboundary side is lengthened and every new boundary side is shortened, so \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

After this refinement the triangulation is face-to-face. Indeed, nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, since two triangles adjacent to crossing nonparallel edges would have overlapping interiors near the crossing. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then, because both join \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), they have the same endpoints and are the same edge. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by the subdivision. From now on, write \(\mathcal T\) for this refined triangulation.

---

### 2. Descent to a finite PL embedded Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
The Euclidean metric on \(\Sigma\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant, so it induces a flat metric on \(M_\beta\). Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The realization condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need the quotient cell structure to be finite and regular. First, every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus for \(k\ne0\),
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge \beta.
\]

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on a refined triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\). Hence there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Therefore \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If
\[
z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\ne0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

Now a compact fundamental rectangle, say \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\), meets only finitely many refined triangles by local finiteness. Since its \(G_\beta\)-translates cover \(\Sigma\), there are only finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Therefore the quotient of \(\mathcal T\) gives a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\). If one wants a literal finite simplicial complex, take its barycentric subdivision; \(F\) remains PL. We keep the clean quotient cellulation for the leaf construction.

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): after subdividing the arc at boundary vertices, each piece lies in a boundary edge on which \(F\) has contraction factor \(<1\).

---

### 3. The leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. The two endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary sides. Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary sides are the same edge of the quotient cellulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), the leaf graph.

Every nonboundary edge lies in the interior of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles: locally, the two sides of the edge must both be filled, and disjoint interiors forbid more than one triangle on either side. Hence every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of the closed triangles whose cevian-intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two extreme boundary edges. Therefore all triangles incident to a boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Hence each \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct similarly the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). The same open-closed argument in the connected strip \(\Sigma\) shows \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, and the quotient map gives a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]
No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, if a leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Thus the covering is infinite, and since it is connected,
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1.1 its image is a nondegenerate segment, so it has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\), depending continuously on the leaf. If \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment with opposite orientation. Hence along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\),
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

---

### 4. Oriented-line degree lemma

We use the following elementary Borsuk-Ulam-type lemma.

**Lemma 4.1.**  
Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]
Then there exist two distinct parameters whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) and extend them by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]

For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one checks
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. But on \(\delta=\tfrac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12),
\]
so the middle-circle map has odd degree. This is impossible because it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map at \(\delta=0\). Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two oriented lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 4.1 to the oriented support-line path from Section 3 gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

---

### 5. The metric \(T\)-pattern estimate

Let the image support lines of \(T\) and \(B\) be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), meeting perpendicularly at \(P\).

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp \ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
because every lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Therefore
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), since otherwise \(Q\in\ell_T\), forcing \(y=0\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the image segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
The two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have flat lengths summing to \(2\beta\). Let \(D\) be the one containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Now choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+h,1).
\]
On the quotient boundary, identify a bottom point \((u,0)\) with coordinate \(u\in\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\), and a top point \((u,1)\) with coordinate \(u-\beta\). Thus the boundary-coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
h-\beta \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), it follows that
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
Therefore
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{5.1}
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Using (5.1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{5.2}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary estimate
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}
\]
applies. Indeed, in coordinates with \(AC\) as the \(x\)-axis and \(|A-C|=L\), the left-hand side is minimized when the projection of \(Q\) is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Thus, using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Strict boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{5.3}
\]

Combining (5.2) and (5.3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]

Finally, for every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower-bound implication. \(\square\)

writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt:
Write a fully self-contained proof of the following theorem. Define every symbol and convention inline. Do not refer to any prior task, solver history, or external construction.

Theorem: Let Σ = R × [0,1], ∂Σ = R × {0,1}, and for β > 0 let Gβ(x,y) = (x+β,1−y). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of ∂Σ. A clean triangulation is countable, locally finite, has disjoint interiors, and covers Σ. It is Gβ-invariant if Gβ preserves it. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into R³ that strictly decreases the length of the boundary edge and strictly increases the lengths of the other two edges. A number β is realized if there is a continuous f:Σ→R³ restricting to a squeeze map on each triangle of some Gβ-invariant clean triangulation and satisfying f(p)=f(q) if and only if p=Gβ^k(q) for some integer k. Prove that the realized set is exactly (√3,∞), and hence its infimum is √3.

Use the following structure.

Part I, lower bound. Prove that if β is realized then β > √3. Include: a cevian lengthening lemma for affine squeeze maps on clean triangles; an equivariant refinement that subdivides boundary edges at all boundary vertices and cones to apices, preserving the squeeze inequalities; descent to the quotient Möbius band Mβ = Σ/<Gβ>, whose boundary has flat length 2β, and proof that the quotient PL cellulation is finite and embedded; construction of the leaf graph from all apex-to-boundary cevians and proof that the quotient leaf graph is a single circle while the lifted leaf graph is a line; proof that orienting lifted leaves from y=0 to y=1 gives a continuous path of oriented support lines whose endpoint is the opposite oriented initial line; an oriented-line lemma saying such a path contains two distinct perpendicular intersecting support lines, with proof via direction and moment coordinates or an equivalent Borsuk-Ulam/degree argument; and the metric estimate. For the metric estimate, choose the two leaves T and B, relabel so B lies in one ray from the intersection point, choose endpoint q of B farther from the intersection, define the boundary arcs D and H between endpoints of T with lengths β+t and β−t, handle possible winding by proving the lifted horizontal displacement h of T satisfies h ≡ ±t mod 2β and hence |h| ≥ |t|, then derive β > sqrt(1+t²)+t and β > sqrt(5+t²)−t. Finish by showing max{sqrt(1+t²)+t, sqrt(5+t²)−t} ≥ √3 for all real t.

Part II, upper bound. For every β > √3 give the explicit 9-periodic PL construction. Set W = 2β/3 and L = sqrt(1+β²/9). Choose σ with L < σ < W, let Δ = min{σ−L, W−σ}, and choose small λ > 0. Define periodic boundary increments ℓ_j by ℓ_j = W−2λ for j≡2 mod 3 and ℓ_j=λ otherwise, with c_0=0 and c_{j+1}−c_j=ℓ_j, so c_{j+9}=c_j+2β. Define X_j=(c_j,0), Y_j=(c_{j+5}−β,1), quadrilaterals Q_j=conv{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j}, and split Q_j into D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j) and E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}). Prove these clean triangles tile Σ and are Gβ-invariant, with Gβ(D_j)=E_{j+4} and Gβ(E_j)=D_{j+5}. Explain that the quotient has nine vertices v_j modulo 9 and faces T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).

Then construct the target vertices. Let p_0,p_1,p_2 be an equilateral triangle of side σ with centroid o. For small η,h>0 define q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i−o)+z e_3, and set A_i=q_i(1,0), M_i=q_i(1−η,h), C_i=q_i(1+η,2h). Define P_0,...,P_8 by P_0=C_0, P_1=M_0, P_2=A_0, P_3=A_1, P_4=C_1, P_5=M_1, P_6=M_2, P_7=A_2, P_8=C_2. Map v_j to P_j and extend affinely over T_j. Prove embeddedness of the nine target triangles. Include the horizontal-slice argument in barycentric coordinates: for a homothetic triangle of scale r, T(r) is given by λ_i ≥ (1−r)/3 and its side S_ij(r) has λ_k=(1−r)/3; adjacent sides S_ij(r) and S_jk(s) are disjoint if r≠s. List the three side panels and their slice scales, then check the ranges 0<z<h, h<z<2h, and the levels z=0,h,2h. Conclude the target is an embedded PL Möbius band.

Finally verify the squeeze inequalities. Boundary edges of length λ map to very short same-cluster edges, and the long boundary edges of length W−2λ map to edges of length below σ+Δ/4 < W−2λ. For nonboundary edges, compute d_j=c_{j+5}−c_j, obtaining d_j=W+2λ for j≡0 mod 3 and d_j=2W−λ for j≡1,2 mod 3, so |d_j−β|<β/3 and the flat nonboundary lengths are less than L. Since every target nonboundary edge joins different base clusters, its length is greater than σ−Δ/4 > L. Therefore every face map is a squeeze map. The lifted map satisfies f∘Gβ=f and descends to the embedded quotient, so f(p)=f(q) exactly for points in the same Gβ-orbit. This realizes β. Combine with Part I to conclude the theorem.

## The lower-bound theorem

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. We prove that  
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]

Let \(\mathcal T_0\) be a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) a realizing map. All length inequalities below are strict.

---

### 1. Cevian lengthening and a harmless PL refinement

First record the basic metric fact used repeatedly.

**Lemma 1.1 — Cevian lengthening.**  
Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle whose boundary edge is \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le 1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
The two nonboundary sides are lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is concave, hence lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Therefore
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]
For a boundary subsegment, affineness gives a constant contraction factor
\[
\frac{|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|}{|r_1-r_0|}<1,
\]
so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) to remove all PL non-face-to-face degeneracies. For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of \(\mathcal T_0\) lying on that boundary edge, and cone those subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so each original triangle is subdivided into finitely many clean triangles.

The refinement is \(G_\beta\)-invariant, locally finite, and countable. By Lemma 1.1, every new nonboundary side is lengthened and every new boundary side is shortened, so \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

After this refinement the triangulation is face-to-face. Indeed, nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, since two triangles adjacent to crossing nonparallel edges would have overlapping interiors near the crossing. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then, because both join \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), they have the same endpoints and are the same edge. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by the subdivision. From now on, write \(\mathcal T\) for this refined triangulation.

---

### 2. Descent to a finite PL embedded Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
The Euclidean metric on \(\Sigma\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant, so it induces a flat metric on \(M_\beta\). Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The realization condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need the quotient cell structure to be finite and regular. First, every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus for \(k\ne0\),
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge \beta.
\]

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on a refined triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\). Hence there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Therefore \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If
\[
z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\ne0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

Now a compact fundamental rectangle, say \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\), meets only finitely many refined triangles by local finiteness. Since its \(G_\beta\)-translates cover \(\Sigma\), there are only finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Therefore the quotient of \(\mathcal T\) gives a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\). If one wants a literal finite simplicial complex, take its barycentric subdivision; \(F\) remains PL. We keep the clean quotient cellulation for the leaf construction.

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): after subdividing the arc at boundary vertices, each piece lies in a boundary edge on which \(F\) has contraction factor \(<1\).

---

### 3. The leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. The two endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary sides. Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary sides are the same edge of the quotient cellulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), the leaf graph.

Every nonboundary edge lies in the interior of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles: locally, the two sides of the edge must both be filled, and disjoint interiors forbid more than one triangle on either side. Hence every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of the closed triangles whose cevian-intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two extreme boundary edges. Therefore all triangles incident to a boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Hence each \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct similarly the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). The same open-closed argument in the connected strip \(\Sigma\) shows \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, and the quotient map gives a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]
No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, if a leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Thus the covering is infinite, and since it is connected,
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1.1 its image is a nondegenerate segment, so it has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\), depending continuously on the leaf. If \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment with opposite orientation. Hence along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\),
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

---

### 4. Oriented-line degree lemma

We use the following elementary Borsuk-Ulam-type lemma.

**Lemma 4.1.**  
Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]
Then there exist two distinct parameters whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) and extend them by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]

For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one checks
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. But on \(\delta=\tfrac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12),
\]
so the middle-circle map has odd degree. This is impossible because it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map at \(\delta=0\). Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two oriented lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 4.1 to the oriented support-line path from Section 3 gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

---

### 5. The metric \(T\)-pattern estimate

Let the image support lines of \(T\) and \(B\) be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), meeting perpendicularly at \(P\).

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp \ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
because every lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Therefore
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), since otherwise \(Q\in\ell_T\), forcing \(y=0\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the image segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
The two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have flat lengths summing to \(2\beta\). Let \(D\) be the one containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Now choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+h,1).
\]
On the quotient boundary, identify a bottom point \((u,0)\) with coordinate \(u\in\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\), and a top point \((u,1)\) with coordinate \(u-\beta\). Thus the boundary-coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
h-\beta \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), it follows that
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
Therefore
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{5.1}
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Using (5.1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{5.2}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary estimate
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}
\]
applies. Indeed, in coordinates with \(AC\) as the \(x\)-axis and \(|A-C|=L\), the left-hand side is minimized when the projection of \(Q\) is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Thus, using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Strict boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{5.3}
\]

Combining (5.2) and (5.3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]

Finally, for every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower-bound implication. \(\square\)

## Referee verdict

I find **no fatal gap** in the combined t13+t14 proof. After checking the indexing, tilings, quotient identifications, embeddedness arguments, and lower-bound topology, the proof does establish the stronger statement

\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Thus the infimum of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\), and it is not attained.

There are a few places where the exposition should be tightened, but they are minor and repairable by adding the clarifications below.

---

## Upper construction t13: checked points

### 1. Strip tiling and indexing

The length sequence satisfies

\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3,
\end{cases}
\qquad W=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\]

so every \(3\)-block sums to \(W\), and

\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

The quadrilateral slices satisfy

\[
x_{j+1}(t)-x_j(t)=(1-t)\ell_j+t\ell_{j+5}>0,
\]

so the \(Q_j\) tile every horizontal line by consecutive intervals. Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\), the tiling is locally finite and covers all of \(\Sigma\). Splitting \(Q_j\) along \(X_{j+1}Y_j\) gives clean triangles \(D_j,E_j\).

The deck-action formulas are correct:

\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]

Hence

\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]

Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\) in the quotient, so

\[
D_j\mapsto T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad
E_j\mapsto T_{j+5}.
\]

No indexing error appears here.

### 2. Target nine-triangle surface

The face list

\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5})
\]

does give exactly the nine target triangles listed in t13.

The embeddedness argument using horizontal slices is sound. The key barycentric fact is:

\[
T(r)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\},
\]

and adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r)\), \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), meeting only in the common vertex if \(r=s\). This correctly rules out unwanted intersections among the three side panels.

Minor clarification to add: the three side panels are planar convex trapezoids because they connect two parallel homothetic side segments. Their horizontal slices are exactly the claimed side segments \(S_{01}(r)\), \(S_{12}(r)\), \(S_{20}(r)\). Therefore the slice check proves that all target triangles meet only in the quotient-prescribed edges and vertices.

### 3. Length inequalities

Boundary edges:

- If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat length is \(\lambda\), and the target length is \(<\lambda/2\).
- If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat length is \(W-2\lambda\), and the target length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4<W-2\lambda\).

Nonboundary edges:

For \(e_j=v_jv_{j+5}\), using the lift \(X_jY_j\),

\[
|X_jY_j|^2=1+(d_j-\beta)^2,
\qquad
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]

The computation

\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3
\end{cases}
\]

implies

\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3},
\]

so every flat nonboundary edge has length \(<L=\sqrt{1+\beta^2/9}\). The opposite lift \(X_{j+1}Y_j\) represents \(e_{j+5}\) and gives the same estimate. All target nonboundary edges join different clusters, so their target lengths are \(>\sigma-\Delta/4>L\). Thus all nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened.

### 4. Deck-orbit condition

Because the target quotient map \(F\) is embedded and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), one gets

\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]

for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). This part is correct once the embeddedness of the quotient surface is stated explicitly.

---

## Lower bound t14: checked points

### 1. Cevian lengthening and refinement

The cevian lemma is correct. The function

\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2
\]

is strictly positive at \(s=0,1\) and strictly concave because the boundary edge is shortened. Hence every cevian is strictly lengthened.

The equivariant boundary refinement is legitimate. Local finiteness implies only finitely many subdivision points on each compact boundary edge. After subdivision, boundary vertex-in-edge incidences disappear, nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, and collinear nonboundary edges must coincide because both run from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Thus the triangulation may be assumed face-to-face.

### 2. Quotient embedded Möbius band

The realization condition gives \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to an injective continuous map

\[
F:M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\to\mathbb R^3.
\]

Since \(M_\beta\) is compact, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

The argument proving finiteness of the quotient cellulation is also sound. A nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\), so an affine face map cannot fail to be injective on a triangle. Therefore no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself, and the quotient has no self-identified faces or edges. A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, giving a finite regular PL cellulation.

Minor clarification to add: arbitrary boundary arcs should be subdivided also at their endpoints and at boundary vertices; then strict shortening follows piecewise.

### 3. Leaf graph

The leaf graph construction is valid. Each triangle contributes an interval of cevians, and endpoints are glued across common nonboundary edges. Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every leaf-graph vertex has valence \(2\).

The connectedness proof is essentially correct. For a component \(C\), the union \(U_C\) of corresponding closed triangles is open because across nonboundary edges and boundary-vertex fans all adjacent triangles remain in the same component. It is closed because the quotient complex is finite. Thus the finite leaf graph is connected and \(2\)-regular, hence a circle.

For the lifted graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), the same argument works, with one extra sentence: local finiteness implies that a union of a subcollection of closed triangles is closed. Thus \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. Since no nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf, the covering

\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]

is infinite, and therefore

\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

The orientation reversal statement is correct: orienting leaves from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), the deck transformation \(G_\beta\) reverses the image segment orientation because it swaps the two boundary components while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\).

### 4. Oriented-line lemma

The oriented-line degree lemma is sound. The anti-periodic extension

\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s))
\]

makes the functions

\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]

\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s)
\]

well-defined on \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\). The involution

\[
(s,\delta)\mapsto(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]

sends \((a,b)\) to \(-(a,b)\). If \((a,b)\) never vanished, the normalized map to \(S^1\) would have degree \(0\) on \(\delta=0\) but odd degree on \(\delta=1/2\), contradiction.

At a zero, \(a=0\) gives perpendicular directions, and \(b=0\) gives coplanarity, hence the two nonparallel support lines intersect.

### 5. Metric \(T\)-pattern estimate

The metric estimate is correct. The one minor point worth spelling out is that the endpoints of the chosen leaf \(T\) are distinct on the quotient boundary; otherwise \(F(T)\) would be degenerate, contradicting cevian lengthening.

Given perpendicular intersecting support lines for leaves \(T,B\), at least one image segment does not contain the intersection point \(P\) in its relative interior. Relabel it as \(B\). Choosing the endpoint \(q\) of \(B\) farther from \(P\) gives

\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(F(q),\ell_T)>1.
\]

Let the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have flat lengths

\[
\beta+t,\qquad \beta-t.
\]

If a lift of \(T\) has endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+h,1)\), then the quotient boundary coordinate difference is \(h-\beta\), so

\[
h\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta},
\]

and hence

\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]

Therefore

\[
L=|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

Boundary shortening on the arc \(H\) not containing \(q\) gives

\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]

so

\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t.
\]

Boundary shortening on the arc \(D\) containing \(q\) gives

\[
\beta+t>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]

Since \(Q\) has distance \(y>1\) from the line \(AC\),

\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
>
\sqrt{L^2+4}
>
\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]

Thus

\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t.
\]

Finally,

\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3
\]

for every real \(t\), with equality at \(t=1/\sqrt3\). Hence every realized \(\beta\) satisfies

\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

---

## Final theorem outline

For \(\beta>\sqrt3\), t13 gives an explicit \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation and an embedded PL Möbius-band target surface. The boundary edges are strictly shortened and all transverse edges are strictly lengthened, so the lifted map realizes \(\beta\).

Conversely, if \(\beta\) is realized, t14 refines the triangulation, descends to a finite embedded PL Möbius band, constructs the circle of cevian leaves, applies the oriented-line degree lemma to obtain a perpendicular intersecting pair of support lines, and then uses the boundary-shortening/leaf-lengthening metric estimate to force \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Therefore

\[
\{\text{realized }\beta\}=(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]

and in particular the infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

## Theorem — upper bound

For every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), \(\beta\) is realized by a \(9\)-periodic clean PL construction.

### Proof

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), one has \(L<W\). Choose
\[
L<\sigma<W,
\qquad
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L,\;W-\sigma\}>0,
\]
and choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min(\Delta/20,\beta/12).
\]

Define the boundary lengths
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then each \(3\)-block sums to \(W\), and each \(9\)-block sums to \(3W=2\beta\). Let \(c_0=0\), \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\), so \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\). Define
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]

For each \(j\), let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(t\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(t)=(1-t)c_j+t(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(t)-x_j(t)=(1-t)\ell_j+t\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus the intervals \([x_j(t),x_{j+1}(t)]\) tile each horizontal line, and the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\). Splitting \(Q_j\) by the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\) gives clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]

The \(G_\beta\)-action satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5},
\]
so the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) be the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), indices are modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
and \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has exactly the claimed nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

Now choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+z e_3.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\) sufficiently small. Let
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define the nine target vertices
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
For \(\eta,h\) small enough, every target distance between different base clusters is in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\ \sigma+\Delta/4),
\]
and every same-cluster boundary edge has length \(<\lambda/2\).

Define the quotient PL map \(F\) by \(F(v_j)=P_j\), affine on each
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]
The nine target faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

The three non-horizontal quadrilateral panels are
\[
Q_{01}=F_0\cup F_4,\qquad
Q_{12}=F_3\cup F_7,\qquad
Q_{20}=F_1\cup F_6.
\]
They connect respectively the sides \(01\), \(12\), \(20\).

To verify embeddedness rigorously, use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\). For the homothetic triangle of scale \(r\),
\[
T(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\},
\]
one has
\[
T(r)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r)\) opposite \(k\) is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r)=\{\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\}\cap T(r).
\]
Hence adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only in the common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then \(S_{ij}(r)\cap T(s)=\varnothing\).

The horizontal sections are exactly:
\[
Q_{01}\cap\{z\} = S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right),
\quad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
Q_{12}\cap\{z\} = S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{z}{2h}\right),
\quad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
Q_{20}\cap\{z\} = S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{z}{h}\right),
\quad 0\le z\le h.
\]

Now check slices.

- For \(0<z<h\), only \(Q_{12}\) and \(Q_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
  \[
  1+\eta\frac z{2h}>1,\qquad 1-\eta\frac zh<1,
  \]
  so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

- For \(h<z<2h\), only \(Q_{01}\) and \(Q_{12}\) occur. Their scales satisfy
  \[
  \left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
  -
  \left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
  =
  \frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
  \]
  so again the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

- At \(z=0\), the horizontal triangle \([A_0,A_1,A_2]\) meets \(Q_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and \(Q_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\), exactly as intended.

- At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \([M_0,M_1,M_2]\) meets \(Q_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and \(Q_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\). The extra section of \(Q_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), while the middle triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), so that section lies outside the middle triangle and is disjoint from it.

- At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \([C_0,C_1,C_2]\) meets \(Q_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and \(Q_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\), exactly as intended.

Each panel \(Q_{01},Q_{12},Q_{20}\) is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles in each panel meet only in that diagonal. Therefore the nine target triangles intersect only in their prescribed common edges and vertices. In particular, the boundary polygon
\[
C_0,M_0,A_0,A_1,C_1,M_1,M_2,A_2,C_2,C_0
\]
is simple. Thus \(F\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

Now define the lifted map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with \(P_{j+9}=P_j\), and extend affinely over every \(D_j,E_j\). Then
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Hence \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\). Consequently
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

It remains to check the squeeze inequalities. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and its target length is \(<\lambda/2\). If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), while the target edge joins different clusters, so its length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4\). Since \(\sigma\le W-\Delta\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

For a nonboundary quotient edge \(e_j=v_jv_{j+5}\), use the lift \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
Then the flat length is
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct computation gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3},
\]
so every nonboundary flat edge has length \(<L\).

The opposite lift gives the same length: the edge \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), which represents \(e_{j+5}\), has horizontal displacement
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it is covered by the same estimate.

Every target nonboundary edge joins different base clusters, so its length is \(>\sigma-\Delta/4\). Since \(\sigma\ge L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L.
\]
Therefore every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Thus on every clean triangle, the boundary edge is shortened and the two nonboundary edges are lengthened. Hence each affine face map is a squeeze map. Therefore \(\beta\) is realized. \(\square\)

Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.]
